Payoffs encode preferences: player \(\) prefers action profile \(\) profile \('\) \(u_i() > u_i(')\).Payoffs. function \(u_i : \\mathbb{R}\) assigns numerical reward player \(\) every possible combination actions. Payoffs encode preferences: player \(\) prefers action profile \(\) profile \('\) \(u_i() > u_i(')\).Information. player knows choosing action. simultaneous game, players choose without observing others’ moves; sequential game, later movers may observe earlier choices. explore sequential information structures 6.Information. player knows choosing action. simultaneous game, players choose without observing others’ moves; sequential game, later movers may observe earlier choices. explore sequential information structures 6.compact way represent two-player simultaneous game payoff matrix (also called normal form). row corresponds action Player 1, column action Player 2, cell contains pair \((u_1, u_2)\) payoffs. study representation depth 3.

heading: 2.2.2 What makes a situation a “game”?
text: every decision problem game. hiker choosing trail based weather forecasts faces uncertainty, weather “respond” hiker’s choice. game requires least two purposeful agents whose payoffs mutually dependent. interdependence key criterion: best action one player depends action chosen another, situation strategic thus game (Shoham & Leyton-Brown, 2009).

heading: 2.2.3 Three canonical examples
text: Prisoner’s Dilemma. Two suspects interrogated separately. can Cooperate (stay silent) Defect (betray ). Mutual cooperation yields moderate reward , mutual defection yields poor outcome , unilateral defection gives defector high payoff cooperator’s expense. tragedy player dominant incentive defect, even though mutual cooperation leave better . game central study cooperation revisited 8 18. payoff structure :Coordination (Stag Hunt). Two hunters can chase Stag (requires joint effort) Hare (can caught alone). hunt stag, feast; one hunts alone, stag escapes hunter gets nothing catches hare. game two pure-strategy equilibria highlights tension efficiency safety.Zero-sum (Matching Pennies). player secretly shows Heads Tails. coins match, Player 1 wins; otherwise Player 2 wins. Every gain one player equal loss , \(u_1() + u_2() = 0\) every profile \(\). class games Neumann & Morgenstern (1944) first solved completely. study mixed-strategy solutions 5.

heading: 2.3 Implementation in R
text:
now build R function create visualize 2x2 payoff matrix. function returns tidy data frame suitable plotting ggplot2.Next build data frames three canonical games combine one tidy dataset.Now produce publication-quality heatmap three payoff matrices, using Player 1’s payoff fill colour.
Figure 2.1: Payoff matrices three canonical 2x2 games. Cell colour encodes Player 1’s payoff using diverging Okabe-Ito-inspired palette; cell displays players’ payoffs ordered pair.
code:
# Build a tidy data frame for a 2x2 payoff matrix
make_payoff_df <- function(game_name, row_actions, col_actions, payoffs) {
  # payoffs: list of four pairs c(u1, u2) in row-major order
  tibble(
    game       = game_name,
    row_action = rep(row_actions, each = length(col_actions)),
    col_action = rep(col_actions, times = length(row_actions)),
    payoff_1   = map_dbl(payoffs, 1),
    payoff_2   = map_dbl(payoffs, 2),
    label      = glue("({payoff_1}, {payoff_2})")
  )
}
pd <- make_payoff_df(
  "Prisoner's Dilemma",
  c("Cooperate", "Defect"), c("Cooperate", "Defect"),
  list(c(3, 3), c(0, 5), c(5, 0), c(1, 1))
)

stag <- make_payoff_df(
  "Stag Hunt",
  c("Stag", "Hare"), c("Stag", "Hare"),
  list(c(4, 4), c(0, 3), c(3, 0), c(2, 2))
)

mp <- make_payoff_df(
  "Matching Pennies",
  c("Heads", "Tails"), c("Heads", "Tails"),
  list(c(1, -1), c(-1, 1), c(-1, 1), c(1, -1))
)

games <- bind_rows(pd, stag, mp) |>
  mutate(
    game = factor(game, levels = c("Prisoner's Dilemma",
                                   "Stag Hunt",
                                   "Matching Pennies"))
  )
p_heatmap <- ggplot(games, aes(x = col_action, y = row_action,
                                fill = payoff_1)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), size = 3.6, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ game, scales = "free") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = okabe_ito[6],   # vermillion for negative/low
    mid = "white",
    high = okabe_ito[3],  # bluish-green for positive/high
    midpoint = 1.5,
    name = "Player 1\npayoff"
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  labs(x = "Player 2", y = "Player 1") +
  theme_publication(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank()
  )

p_heatmap

heading: 3 Normal-Form Games
text: normal (strategic) form workhorse representation simultaneous-move games. chapter introduces payoff matrices, strict weak dominance, iterated elimination strictly dominated strategies (IESDS), four classic 2x2 games recur throughout game theory.

heading: Learning objectives

heading: 3.1 Motivation
text: Imagine two rival coffee shops opening street, choosing price latte – high low – without knowing ’s decision. shop’s profit depends price also competitor’s. charge high prices, share comfortable market; one undercuts , cheaper shop captures customers; go low, margins evaporate everyone.kind simultaneous decision strategic interdependence exactly normal-form (strategic-form) representation designed capture. Introduced Neumann & Morgenstern (1944), normal form strips game essentials: players, can player , payoff combination choices produce? elements arranged payoff matrix, makes strategic structure visible glance starting point almost every solution concept non-cooperative game theory.

heading: 3.2.1 The normal-form representation
text: finite normal-form game tuple \(G = (N, (S_i)_{\N}, (u_i)_{\N})\) \(N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}\) set players,\(S_i\) finite set strategies (actions) available player \(\), \(u_i : S_1 \times \cdots \times S_n \\mathbb{R}\) player \(\)’s payoff function.two-player game Player 1 \(m\) strategies Player 2 \(k\) strategies, arrange payoffs \(m \times k\) matrix. cell \((, j)\) contains pair \((u_1(s_i, s_j),\; u_2(s_i, s_j))\). Player 1 chooses row, Player 2 chooses column, cell intersection determines payoffs.

heading: 3.2.2 Dominance
3.2.3 Iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies (IESDS)
text: strategy strictly dominated, rational player never use . Knowing , players can remove strategy consideration, potentially revealing new dominated strategies reduced game. Repeating process called IESDS (iterated elimination strictly dominated strategies).key property IESDS order independence: final set surviving strategies regardless order dominated strategies removed (Osborne, 2004, Chapter 2). IESDS reduces game single strategy profile, game dominance solvable – Prisoner’s Dilemma famous example.

heading: 3.2.4 Classic 2x2 games
text: Four canonical 2x2 games appear throughout game theory applications. represents different type strategic tension:Prisoner’s Dilemma. player dominant strategy (Defect), mutual cooperation make better . unique Nash equilibrium Pareto-inefficient.Stag Hunt. Two pure-strategy equilibria exist: one payoff-dominant (hunt Stag) one risk-dominant (hunt Hare). game models trade-trust safety.Chicken (Hawk-Dove). Two asymmetric pure equilibria (one swerves, ) plus mixed equilibrium. captures brinkmanship anti-coordination.Battle Sexes. Two pure equilibria players coordinate different preferred outcomes, plus mixed equilibrium. models coordination conflicting preferences.games reappear 4 equilibrium analysis, 5 mixed-strategy computation, 8 study cooperation time.

heading: 3.3 Implementation in R
text: begin building payoff matrices four classic games writing helper function check strictly dominated strategies.
code:
# Define the four classic 2x2 games as named lists
# Each entry: list(A = Player 1 payoffs, B = Player 2 payoffs)
classic_games <- list(
  "Prisoner's Dilemma" = list(
    A = matrix(c(3, 0, 5, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("C", "D"), c("C", "D"))),
    B = matrix(c(3, 5, 0, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("C", "D"), c("C", "D")))
  ),
  "Stag Hunt" = list(
    A = matrix(c(4, 0, 3, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Stag", "Hare"), c("Stag", "Hare"))),
    B = matrix(c(4, 3, 0, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Stag", "Hare"), c("Stag", "Hare")))
  ),
  "Chicken" = list(
    A = matrix(c(0, -1, 1, -5), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Swerve", "Straight"), c("Swerve", "Straight"))),
    B = matrix(c(0, 1, -1, -5), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Swerve", "Straight"), c("Swerve", "Straight")))
  ),
  "Battle of the Sexes" = list(
    A = matrix(c(3, 0, 0, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Opera", "Football"), c("Opera", "Football"))),
    B = matrix(c(2, 0, 0, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Opera", "Football"), c("Opera", "Football")))
  )
)

heading: 3.3.1 Checking for strict dominance
3.3.2 Publication figure: classic 2x2 payoff structures
text: Figure 3.1: Payoff structures four classic 2x2 games. cell shows Player 1’s payoff. colour gradient highlights incentive landscape: brighter cells attractive row player.
code:
# Convert all four games into a long tibble for faceted plotting
games_long <- purrr::imap_dfr(classic_games, function(game, name) {
  mat <- game$A
  tidyr::expand_grid(
    row = rownames(mat),
    col = colnames(mat)
  ) |>
    mutate(
      payoff = purrr::map2_dbl(row, col, ~ mat[.x, .y]),
      game = name
    )
})

# Preserve strategy label ordering
games_long <- games_long |>
  mutate(
    row = factor(row, levels = rev(c("C", "D", "Stag", "Hare",
                                     "Swerve", "Straight",
                                     "Opera", "Football"))),
    col = factor(col, levels = c("C", "D", "Stag", "Hare",
                                 "Swerve", "Straight",
                                 "Opera", "Football")),
    game = factor(game, levels = c("Prisoner's Dilemma", "Stag Hunt",
                                   "Chicken", "Battle of the Sexes"))
  )

p_classic <- ggplot(games_long, aes(x = col, y = row, fill = payoff)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = payoff), size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ game, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = okabe_ito[6], mid = okabe_ito[4], high = okabe_ito[3],
    midpoint = 1.5, name = "Player 1\npayoff"
  ) +
  labs(x = "Player 2", y = "Player 1",
       title = "Payoff Structures of Classic 2×2 Games") +
  theme_publication(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10)
  )

p_classic
save_pub_fig(p_classic, "classic-2x2-payoffs", width = 8, height = 6)

heading: 3.4 Worked example
text: apply IESDS step step 3x3 game illustrate procedure works.Consider following game Player 1 chooses row (T, M, B) Player 2 chooses column (L, C, R):Step 1. Check Player 1’s strategies dominance. Compare row B row M: \(M\) gives \((4, 3, 3)\) versus \(B\)’s \((3, 0, 2)\). Since \(4 > 3\), \(3 > 0\), \(3 > 2\), strategy M strictly dominates B. Eliminate B.Step 2. reduced 2x3 game, check Player 2’s strategies. Compare column C column R Player 2: C gives \((1, 4)\) versus R’s \((0, 1)\). Column C strictly dominates column R. Eliminate R.Step 3. 2x2 game, check Player 1. Row M gives \((4, 3)\) versus row T’s \((1, 2)\). M strictly dominates T. Eliminate T.Step 4. Player 2 now chooses L (payoff 1) C (payoff 4). C strictly dominates L. unique surviving profile (M, C) payoffs (3, 4).game dominance solvable: IESDS reduces unique strategy profile. outcome must Nash equilibrium – fact, IESDS can eliminate strategies part Nash equilibrium, surviving profile always Nash equilibrium (Osborne, 2004).
code:
# Player 1 payoff matrix
A3 <- matrix(
  c(1, 2, 5,
    4, 3, 3,
    3, 0, 2),
  nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE,
  dimnames = list(c("T", "M", "B"), c("L", "C", "R"))
)

# Player 2 payoff matrix
B3 <- matrix(
  c(2, 1, 0,
    1, 4, 1,
    3, 2, 3),
  nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE,
  dimnames = list(c("T", "M", "B"), c("L", "C", "R"))
)

cat("Player 1 payoffs:\n")#> Player 1 payoffs:
A3#>   L C R
#> T 1 2 5
#> M 4 3 3
#> B 3 0 2
cat("\nPlayer 2 payoffs:\n")#> 
#> Player 2 payoffs:
B3#>   L C R
#> T 2 1 0
#> M 1 4 1
#> B 3 2 3
cat("Step 1: M strictly dominates B for Player 1.\n")#> Step 1: M strictly dominates B for Player 1.
cat("  M payoffs: ", A3["M", ], "\n")#>   M payoffs:  4 3 3
cat("  B payoffs: ", A3["B", ], "\n")#>   B payoffs:  3 0 2
cat("  All M > B? ", all(A3["M", ] > A3["B", ]), "\n")#>   All M > B?  TRUE
# Reduce the game
A_r1 <- A3[c("T", "M"), ]
B_r1 <- B3[c("T", "M"), ]
cat("\nReduced game (Player 1 payoffs):\n")#> 
#> Reduced game (Player 1 payoffs):
A_r1#>   L C R
#> T 1 2 5
#> M 4 3 3
cat("Step 2: C strictly dominates R for Player 2.\n")#> Step 2: C strictly dominates R for Player 2.
cat("  C payoffs (P2): ", B_r1[, "C"], "\n")#>   C payoffs (P2):  1 4
cat("  R payoffs (P2): ", B_r1[, "R"], "\n")#>   R payoffs (P2):  0 1
cat("  All C > R? ", all(B_r1[, "C"] > B_r1[, "R"]), "\n")#>   All C > R?  TRUE
# Reduce further
A_r2 <- A_r1[, c("L", "C")]
B_r2 <- B_r1[, c("L", "C")]
cat("\nReduced game (Player 1 payoffs):\n")#> 
#> Reduced game (Player 1 payoffs):
A_r2#>   L C
#> T 1 2
#> M 4 3
cat("\nReduced game (Player 2 payoffs):\n")#> 
#> Reduced game (Player 2 payoffs):
B_r2#>   L C
#> T 2 1
#> M 1 4
cat("Step 3: M strictly dominates T for Player 1.\n")#> Step 3: M strictly dominates T for Player 1.
cat("  M payoffs: ", A_r2["M", ], "\n")#>   M payoffs:  4 3
cat("  T payoffs: ", A_r2["T", ], "\n")#>   T payoffs:  1 2
cat("  All M > T? ", all(A_r2["M", ] > A_r2["T", ]), "\n")#>   All M > T?  TRUE
A_r3 <- A_r2["M", , drop = FALSE]
B_r3 <- B_r2["M", , drop = FALSE]
cat("\nReduced game (Player 2 payoffs):\n")#> 
#> Reduced game (Player 2 payoffs):
B_r3#>   L C
#> M 1 4
cat("Step 4: C strictly dominates L for Player 2.\n")#> Step 4: C strictly dominates L for Player 2.
cat("  C payoff: ", B_r3[, "C"], "\n")#>   C payoff:  4
cat("  L payoff: ", B_r3[, "L"], "\n")#>   L payoff:  1
cat("\nIESDS solution: (M, C)\n")#> 
#> IESDS solution: (M, C)
cat(glue("Payoffs: ({A3['M', 'C']}, {B3['M', 'C']})"), "\n")#> Payoffs: (3, 4)

heading: 4 Nash Equilibrium
text: Definition, existence, computation, multiplicity Nash equilibrium — central solution concept non-cooperative game theory.

heading: Learning objectives
text: Define Nash equilibrium terms best responses unilateral deviations.State Nash’s existence theorem understand mixed strategies guarantee existence.Compute pure-strategy mixed-strategy Nash equilibria 2×2 games R.Visualize best-response correspondences identify equilibria graphically.

heading: 4.1 Motivation
text: Consider two competing firms choosing prices simultaneously. firm wants maximize profit, profit depends rival’s price. Firm simply optimize isolation — must reason Firm B’s choice, knowing Firm B reasoning Firm exactly way.mutual consistency requirement heart Nash equilibrium: strategy profile player can improve payoff changing strategy alone, given everyone else . widely used solution concept non-cooperative game theory, starting point almost every applied analysis strategic interaction.

heading: 4.2.1 Best responses
4.2.2 Nash equilibrium defined
4.2.3 Existence
text: Nash (1950) proved every finite game — one finitely many players finitely many actions per player — least one Nash equilibrium, possibly mixed strategies. proof uses Kakutani’s fixed-point theorem applied best-response correspondence.Theorem: Nash’s Existence TheoremEvery finite game least one Nash equilibrium mixed strategies.theorem guarantee pure-strategy equilibrium. game Matching Pennies, instance, pure-strategy equilibrium unique mixed equilibrium player randomizes 50-50 (see 5).

heading: 4.2.4 Multiplicity
text: Games can zero, one, many pure-strategy equilibria:Matching Pennies — zero pure, one mixed.Battle Sexes — two pure, one mixed.Prisoner’s Dilemma — one pure (Defect, Defect), also unique NE overall.multiple equilibria exist, selecting among separate problem — equilibrium selection problem — addressed refinements subgame perfection (6), trembling-hand perfection, risk dominance.

heading: 4.3 Implementation in R
text: implement two approaches: pure-R solver 2×2 games visualization best-response correspondences.

heading: 4.3.1 Finding pure-strategy Nash equilibria
code:
source(here::here("R", "solvers.R"))

# Prisoner's Dilemma payoff matrices
# Rows = Player 1 actions (C, D), Cols = Player 2 actions (C, D)
A <- matrix(c(3, 0, 5, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
            dimnames = list(c("C", "D"), c("C", "D")))
B <- matrix(c(3, 5, 0, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
            dimnames = list(c("C", "D"), c("C", "D")))

cat("Player 1 payoffs:\n")#> Player 1 payoffs:
A#>   C D
#> C 3 0
#> D 5 1
cat("\nPlayer 2 payoffs:\n")#> 
#> Player 2 payoffs:
B#>   C D
#> C 3 5
#> D 0 1
pure_ne <- solve_2x2_pure_nash(A, B)
cat("\nPure-strategy Nash equilibria:\n")#> 
#> Pure-strategy Nash equilibria:
for (eq in pure_ne) {
  cat(sprintf("  (%s, %s) with payoffs (%d, %d)\n",
              rownames(A)[eq[1]], colnames(A)[eq[2]],
              A[eq[1], eq[2]], B[eq[1], eq[2]]))
}#>   (D, D) with payoffs (1, 1)

heading: 4.3.2 Battle of the Sexes — multiple equilibria
code:
# Battle of the Sexes
A_bos <- matrix(c(3, 0, 0, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                dimnames = list(c("Opera", "Football"), c("Opera", "Football")))
B_bos <- matrix(c(2, 0, 0, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                dimnames = list(c("Opera", "Football"), c("Opera", "Football")))

cat("Battle of the Sexes:\n")#> Battle of the Sexes:
cat("Player 1 payoffs:\n")#> Player 1 payoffs:
A_bos#>          Opera Football
#> Opera        3        0
#> Football     0        2
cat("\nPlayer 2 payoffs:\n")#> 
#> Player 2 payoffs:
B_bos#>          Opera Football
#> Opera        2        0
#> Football     0        3
pure_bos <- solve_2x2_pure_nash(A_bos, B_bos)
mixed_bos <- solve_2x2_mixed_nash(A_bos, B_bos)

cat("\nPure-strategy NE:\n")#> 
#> Pure-strategy NE:
for (eq in pure_bos) {
  cat(sprintf("  (%s, %s)\n", rownames(A_bos)[eq[1]], colnames(A_bos)[eq[2]]))
}#>   (Opera, Opera)
#>   (Football, Football)
cat(sprintf("\nMixed-strategy NE: p = %.2f, q = %.2f\n",
            mixed_bos$p, mixed_bos$q))#> 
#> Mixed-strategy NE: p = 0.60, q = 0.40

heading: 4.3.3 Best-response correspondence plot
text: Figure 4.1: Best-response correspondences Battle Sexes. Player 1’s best response (orange) Player 2’s best response (blue) intersect three Nash equilibria: two pure-strategy one mixed.
code:
# For a 2x2 game, let p = P(Player 1 plays row 1), q = P(Player 2 plays col 1)
# Player 2's expected payoff from col 1: p * B[1,1] + (1-p) * B[2,1]
# Player 2's expected payoff from col 2: p * B[1,2] + (1-p) * B[2,2]
# Player 2 is indifferent when these are equal

p_seq <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 200)

# Player 1's best response: BR1(q)
# EU1(row1) = q*A[1,1] + (1-q)*A[1,2] = q*3
# EU1(row2) = q*A[2,1] + (1-q)*A[2,2] = (1-q)*2
# Indifferent when 3q = 2(1-q) => q = 2/5
br1_q <- 2 / (3 + 2)  # q at which P1 is indifferent

# Player 2's best response: BR2(p)
# EU2(col1) = p*B[1,1] + (1-p)*B[2,1] = 2p
# EU2(col2) = p*B[1,2] + (1-p)*B[2,2] = 3(1-p)
# Indifferent when 2p = 3(1-p) => p = 3/5
br2_p <- 3 / (2 + 3)  # p at which P2 is indifferent

# Build best-response data
br_data <- tibble(
  q = c(0, br1_q, br1_q, br1_q, 1),
  p_br1 = c(0, 0, 0.5, 1, 1),
  type = "Player 1 BR"
)

br2_data <- tibble(
  p = c(0, br2_p, br2_p, br2_p, 1),
  q_br2 = c(0, 0, 0.5, 1, 1),
  type = "Player 2 BR"
)

# Equilibrium points
eq_points <- tibble(
  p = c(0, br2_p, 1),
  q = c(0, br1_q, 1),
  label = c("(Football, Football)", "Mixed NE", "(Opera, Opera)")
)

p_fig <- ggplot() +
  geom_path(data = br_data, aes(x = q, y = p_br1),
            colour = okabe_ito[1], linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_path(data = br2_data, aes(x = q_br2, y = p),
            colour = okabe_ito[2], linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(data = eq_points, aes(x = q, y = p),
             size = 3, colour = okabe_ito[6]) +
  geom_text(data = eq_points, aes(x = q, y = p, label = label),
            vjust = -1, size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.85, y = 0.15, label = "BR₁(q)",
           colour = okabe_ito[1], fontface = "bold", size = 4) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.15, y = 0.85, label = "BR₂(p)",
           colour = okabe_ito[2], fontface = "bold", size = 4) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "q  (Player 2: prob. of Opera)",
                     limits = c(-0.05, 1.15), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "p  (Player 1: prob. of Opera)",
                     limits = c(-0.05, 1.25), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  theme_publication() +
  labs(title = "Best-Response Correspondences: Battle of the Sexes")

p_fig
save_pub_fig(p_fig, "best-response-bos")

heading: 4.4 Worked example
text: Consider coordination game two drivers approach road must choose swerve Left Right:coordination game two pure NE — (Left, Left) (Right, Right) — mixed NE \(p = q = 0.5\) expected payoff 0.5, worse either pure equilibrium. illustrates mixed equilibria can Pareto-dominated pure ones: randomization creates risk miscoordination.
code:
A_coord <- matrix(c(1, 0, 0, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                  dimnames = list(c("Left", "Right"), c("Left", "Right")))
B_coord <- A_coord  # symmetric game

cat("Coordination Game (symmetric):\n")#> Coordination Game (symmetric):
A_coord#>       Left Right
#> Left     1     0
#> Right    0     1
eq_coord <- support_enumeration(A_coord, B_coord)

cat("\nPure-strategy NE:\n")#> 
#> Pure-strategy NE:
for (eq in eq_coord$pure) {
  cat(sprintf("  (%s, %s)\n", rownames(A_coord)[eq[1]], colnames(A_coord)[eq[2]]))
}#>   (Left, Left)
#>   (Right, Right)
if (!is.null(eq_coord$mixed)) {
  cat(sprintf("\nMixed NE: p = %.2f, q = %.2f\n",
              eq_coord$mixed$p, eq_coord$mixed$q))
  cat("Expected payoff at mixed NE: ",
      eq_coord$mixed$p * eq_coord$mixed$q * 1 +
      (1 - eq_coord$mixed$p) * (1 - eq_coord$mixed$q) * 1, "\n")
}#> 
#> Mixed NE: p = 0.50, q = 0.50
#> Expected payoff at mixed NE:  0.5

chapter: 5 Mixed Strategies and the Indifference Principle
heading: 5.3.1 Computing mixed equilibria with solve_2x2_mixed_nash()
text: function solve_2x2_mixed_nash() R/solvers.R implements (5.2) (5.3) directly.
code:
# Matching Pennies payoff matrices
A_mp <- matrix(c(1, -1, -1, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Heads", "Tails"), c("Heads", "Tails")))
B_mp <- matrix(c(-1, 1, 1, -1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Heads", "Tails"), c("Heads", "Tails")))

cat("Matching Pennies\n")#> Matching Pennies
cat("Player 1 (Matcher) payoffs:\n")#> Player 1 (Matcher) payoffs:
A_mp#>       Heads Tails
#> Heads     1    -1
#> Tails    -1     1
cat("\nPlayer 2 (Mismatcher) payoffs:\n")#> 
#> Player 2 (Mismatcher) payoffs:
B_mp#>       Heads Tails
#> Heads    -1     1
#> Tails     1    -1
# Verify no pure-strategy NE exists
pure_ne <- solve_2x2_pure_nash(A_mp, B_mp)
cat("\nPure-strategy NE:", length(pure_ne), "\n")#> 
#> Pure-strategy NE: 0
# Compute mixed NE
mixed_ne <- solve_2x2_mixed_nash(A_mp, B_mp)
cat(sprintf("Mixed NE: p = %.2f (P1 plays Heads), q = %.2f (P2 plays Heads)\n",
            mixed_ne$p, mixed_ne$q))#> Mixed NE: p = 0.50 (P1 plays Heads), q = 0.50 (P2 plays Heads)
# Expected payoff at mixed NE
eu_mixed <- mixed_ne$p * mixed_ne$q * A_mp[1,1] +
            mixed_ne$p * (1 - mixed_ne$q) * A_mp[1,2] +
            (1 - mixed_ne$p) * mixed_ne$q * A_mp[2,1] +
            (1 - mixed_ne$p) * (1 - mixed_ne$q) * A_mp[2,2]
cat(sprintf("Player 1 expected payoff at mixed NE: %.2f\n", eu_mixed))#> Player 1 expected payoff at mixed NE: 0.00

chapter: 5 Mixed Strategies and the Indifference Principle
heading: 5.3.2 Visualizing the indifference point
text:
figure plots Player 1’s expected payoff pure action Player 2 varies \(q\), probability playing Heads. indifference point \(q^* = 0.5\), actions yield expected payoff, Player 1 willing mix.
Figure 5.1: Player 1’s expected payoff pure action function Player 2’s mixing probability \(q\) Matching Pennies. lines cross \(q^* = 0.5\), indifference point Player 1 willing randomize.
\(q < 0.5\), Player 2 plays Tails often, Player 1 prefers Tails (orange line ). \(q > 0.5\), Player 1 prefers Heads (blue line ). \(q^* = 0.5\) Player 1 genuinely indifferent therefore willing randomize. logic, applied Player 2’s perspective, pins \(p^* = 0.5\).
code:
q_seq <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 200)

# Player 1's expected payoff from Heads: q * 1 + (1-q) * (-1) = 2q - 1
# Player 1's expected payoff from Tails: q * (-1) + (1-q) * 1 = 1 - 2q
payoff_data <- tibble(
  q = rep(q_seq, 2),
  expected_payoff = c(2 * q_seq - 1, 1 - 2 * q_seq),
  action = rep(c("Heads", "Tails"), each = length(q_seq))
)

# Indifference point
q_star <- 0.5
eu_star <- 2 * q_star - 1  # = 0

p_indiff <- ggplot(payoff_data, aes(x = q, y = expected_payoff, colour = action)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(
    data = tibble(q = q_star, expected_payoff = eu_star),
    aes(x = q, y = expected_payoff),
    colour = okabe_ito[6], size = 4, inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  annotate("text", x = q_star + 0.12, y = eu_star + 0.12,
           label = paste0("Indifference point\n(q* = ", q_star, ")"),
           colour = okabe_ito[6], size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = q_star, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50",
             linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("Heads" = okabe_ito[1], "Tails" = okabe_ito[2]),
    name = "Player 1 action"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "q  (Player 2's probability of Heads)",
                     breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Player 1's expected payoff",
                     breaks = seq(-1, 1, 0.5)) +
  theme_publication() +
  labs(title = "Expected Payoffs and the Indifference Principle")

p_indiff
save_pub_fig(p_indiff, "indifference-matching-pennies")

chapter: 5 Mixed Strategies and the Indifference Principle
heading: 5.4.1 Matching Pennies: step-by-step
text:
Two children play Matching Pennies. simultaneously shows coin Heads Tails. Player 1 (Matcher) wins coins match; Player 2 (Mismatcher) wins differ. payoff matrices defined .Step 1: Check pure-strategy NE. pure-strategy profile, one player can profitably deviate. (Heads, Heads), Player 2 switches Tails. (Heads, Tails), Player 1 switches Tails. profile mutual best response, pure NE exists.Step 2: Apply indifference principle. Player 1 indifferent Heads Tails :\[
q \cdot 1 + (1 - q) \cdot (-1) = q \cdot (-1) + (1 - q) \cdot 1
\]
\[
2q - 1 = 1 - 2q \implies q^* = \tfrac{1}{2}
\]Player 2 indifferent :\[
p \cdot (-1) + (1 - p) \cdot 1 = p \cdot 1 + (1 - p) \cdot (-1)
\]
\[
1 - 2p = 2p - 1 \implies p^* = \tfrac{1}{2}
\]Step 3: State equilibrium. unique Nash equilibrium \((p^*, q^*) = (0.5, 0.5)\). player randomizes uniformly. expected payoff players 0.Step 4: Interpret. equilibrium natural interpretation: player must maximally unpredictable. bias — say, playing Heads 60% time — exploited opponent. mixed NE self-enforcing outcome, consistent Nash’s existence theorem.
code:
# Verify with R
cat("Verification via solve_2x2_mixed_nash():\n")#> Verification via solve_2x2_mixed_nash():
result <- solve_2x2_mixed_nash(A_mp, B_mp)
cat(sprintf("  p* = %.4f, q* = %.4f\n", result$p, result$q))#>   p* = 0.5000, q* = 0.5000
# Full support enumeration
all_eq <- support_enumeration(A_mp, B_mp)
cat(sprintf("  Pure NE: %d\n", length(all_eq$pure)))#>   Pure NE: 0
cat(sprintf("  Mixed NE: p = %.2f, q = %.2f\n", all_eq$mixed$p, all_eq$mixed$q))#>   Mixed NE: p = 0.50, q = 0.50

heading: 6 Extensive-Form Games
text: Game trees, backward induction, subgame perfect equilibrium — tools analyzing sequential strategic interaction timing information matter.

heading: Learning objectives
text: Represent sequential games game trees nodes, branches, information sets.Translate extensive-form normal-form representations game.Apply backward induction solve finite games perfect information.Define subgame perfect equilibrium explain refines Nash equilibrium.Implement backward induction R visualize game trees ggplot2.

heading: 6.1 Motivation
text: normal-form games introduced 3 assume players choose strategies simultaneously, without observing ’s actions. Many real-world interactions, however, unfold time. firm decides whether enter market observing incumbent’s investment capacity. prosecutor makes plea offer defendant decides whether accept . chess player moves response opponent’s prior move.sequence moves matters, need richer representation payoff matrix. extensive form models games trees, node represents decision point, branch represents available action, tree’s structure encodes moves know time decision. representation, introduced Neumann & Morgenstern (1944) refined Kuhn (1953), standard framework analyzing sequential strategic interaction.key payoff moving extensive form sharper solution concept. saw 4, Nash equilibrium can sustain outcomes based threats player never actually carry . Backward induction — reasoning end game back beginning — eliminates non-credible threats leads subgame perfect equilibrium (SPE), refinement requires rational play every subgame, just equilibrium path.

heading: 6.2.1 Game trees
text: extensive-form game consists :set players \(N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}\).game tree — directed tree distinguished root node.player function assigning non-terminal (decision) node player.Actions labeling branches decision node.Information sets partitioning player’s decision nodes groups within player distinguish (reflecting player knows point).Payoff vectors assigned terminal node.game perfect information every information set contains exactly one node — , player knows exactly tree move. Chess, tic-tac-toe, entry deterrence game games perfect information. information set contains multiple nodes, player faces imperfect information prior moves; connects analysis Bayesian games 7.

heading: 6.2.2 Strategies in extensive form vs. normal form
text: strategy extensive form complete contingent plan: specifies action every information set player might called move, including strategy makes unreachable. subtle important distinction normal form. Two extensive-form strategies differ unreachable information sets map normal-form strategy can different implications subgame perfection.extensive-form game can converted normal-form game (see 3) enumerating strategy combinations computing resulting payoffs. However, conversion discards sequential structure, Nash equilibria normal form may include strategy profiles sustained non-credible threats.

heading: 6.2.3 Backward induction
text: finite games perfect information, backward induction provides constructive algorithm finding rational play:Start terminal node record payoffs.Move decision nodes immediately preceding terminal nodes. node, deciding player chooses action maximizes payoff.Replace decision node terminal payoff results optimal choice.Repeat root reached.Theorem: Zermelo–KuhnEvery finite game perfect information least one subgame perfect equilibrium pure strategies, can found backward induction (Osborne, 2004, Chapter 5).

heading: 6.2.4 Subgame perfect equilibrium
text: subgame extensive-form game subtree () begins singleton information set, (ii) contains successors root node, (iii) break information set.Definition: Subgame Perfect EquilibriumA strategy profile subgame perfect equilibrium (SPE) induces Nash equilibrium every subgame game.Every SPE Nash equilibrium, every Nash equilibrium subgame perfect. additional requirement rationality every subgame rules strategies rely non-credible threats — actions player actually carry called upon . refinement central contribution Selten (1965) fundamental analysis sequential games throughout book.

heading: 6.3 Implementation in R
text: implement entry deterrence game — classic example industrial organization — game tree using ggplot2, solve backward induction.

heading: 6.3.1 Defining the game tree
text: Consider entry deterrence game two players:Entrant (Player 1) chooses Enter Stay .Incumbent (Player 2), entry occurs, chooses Accommodate Fight.payoffs : entrant stays , incumbent keeps monopoly (Entrant: 0, Incumbent: 3). entrant enters incumbent accommodates, share market (Entrant: 2, Incumbent: 1). entrant enters incumbent fights, suffer losses (Entrant: -1, Incumbent: -1).
code:
# Define the game tree as a data structure
# Each node: id, player, label, x/y position
nodes <- tibble(
  id     = c("root", "inc", "t1", "t2", "t3"),
  player = c("Entrant", "Incumbent", NA, NA, NA),
  label  = c("Entrant", "Incumbent", "(0, 3)", "(2, 1)", "(-1, -1)"),
  x      = c(0, 1.5, -1.5, 2.5, 0.5),
  y      = c(3, 1.5, 1.5, 0, 0),
  is_terminal = c(FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE)
)

# Edges: from, to, action label
edges <- tibble(
  from_x = c(0, 0, 1.5, 1.5),
  from_y = c(3, 3, 1.5, 1.5),
  to_x   = c(1.5, -1.5, 2.5, 0.5),
  to_y   = c(1.5, 1.5, 0, 0),
  action  = c("Enter", "Stay Out", "Accommodate", "Fight")
)

heading: 6.3.2 Drawing the game tree
text: Figure 6.1: Entry deterrence game solved backward induction. Bold edges show subgame perfect equilibrium path: entrant enters incumbent accommodates. struck-branch (Fight) represents non-credible threat.
code:
# Backward induction result: at the incumbent's node, Accommodate (2,1)
# dominates Fight (-1,-1), so the incumbent accommodates.
# Knowing this, the entrant compares Enter -> (2,1) vs Stay Out -> (0,3):
# Entrant gets 2 > 0, so Enter is optimal.
# SPE path: Enter -> Accommodate

spe_edges <- tibble(
  from_x = c(0, 1.5),
  from_y = c(3, 1.5),
  to_x   = c(1.5, 2.5),
  to_y   = c(1.5, 0)
)

p_tree <- ggplot() +
  # Draw all branches

  geom_segment(data = edges,
               aes(x = from_x, y = from_y, xend = to_x, yend = to_y),
               colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.8) +
  # Highlight SPE path

  geom_segment(data = spe_edges,
               aes(x = from_x, y = from_y, xend = to_x, yend = to_y),
               colour = okabe_ito[3], linewidth = 1.6) +
  # Decision nodes
  geom_point(data = filter(nodes, !is_terminal),
             aes(x = x, y = y),
             shape = 21, size = 6, fill = okabe_ito[2],
             colour = "black", stroke = 1.2) +
  # Terminal nodes
  geom_point(data = filter(nodes, is_terminal),
             aes(x = x, y = y),
             shape = 22, size = 5, fill = okabe_ito[1],
             colour = "black", stroke = 1) +
  # Node labels
  geom_text(data = filter(nodes, !is_terminal),
            aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
            vjust = -1.5, fontface = "bold", size = 3.8) +
  geom_text(data = filter(nodes, is_terminal),
            aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
            vjust = 1.8, size = 3.5) +
  # Action labels on edges
  geom_text(data = edges,
            aes(x = (from_x + to_x) / 2,
                y = (from_y + to_y) / 2,
                label = action),
            nudge_x = c(0.5, -0.6, 0.7, -0.5),
            nudge_y = c(0.15, 0.15, 0.1, 0.1),
            size = 3.3, fontface = "italic",
            colour = c(okabe_ito[3], "grey40", okabe_ito[3], "grey40")) +
  # Annotation for backward induction reasoning
  annotate("label", x = 2.8, y = 1.0,
           label = "Incumbent prefers\n1 > -1: Accommodate",
           size = 2.8, fill = "white", label.size = 0.3,
           colour = okabe_ito[5]) +
  annotate("label", x = -1.5, y = 2.8,
           label = "Entrant prefers\n2 > 0: Enter",
           size = 2.8, fill = "white", label.size = 0.3,
           colour = okabe_ito[5]) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-2.5, 4), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-0.8, 4), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(title = "Backward Induction in the Entry Deterrence Game") +
  theme_publication() +
  theme(
    axis.text  = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )

p_tree
save_pub_fig(p_tree, "entry-deterrence-backward-induction", width = 7, height = 5)

heading: 6.3.3 Backward induction algorithm
6.4 Worked example
text: now trace entry deterrence game step step see exactly backward induction eliminates non-credible threats.Step 1: Identify last decision node. incumbent’s node reached entrant enters. node, incumbent two choices:Accommodate: payoff Incumbent = 1Fight: payoff Incumbent = -1Since \(1 > -1\), rational incumbent chooses Accommodate. Fighting non-credible threat — incumbent never carry actually called upon decide.Step 2: Fold back first decision node. Knowing incumbent accommodate upon entry, entrant faces effective choice :Stay : payoff Entrant = 0Enter: payoff Entrant = 2 (since incumbent accommodate)Since \(2 > 0\), entrant enters.Step 3: subgame perfect equilibrium. SPE strategy profile (Enter, Accommodate), yielding payoffs \((2, 1)\).normal form reveals two Nash equilibria: (Enter, Accommodate) (Stay , Fight). second equilibrium involves incumbent threatening fight entry occurs — threat deters entrant. showed threat non-credible: incumbent actually carry , prefer accommodate. Subgame perfection eliminates (Stay , Fight), leaving (Enter, Accommodate) unique SPE. power backward induction refinement Nash equilibrium.
code:
# Convert to normal form to show the Nash equilibrium issue
# Entrant: {Enter, Stay Out}
# Incumbent: {Accommodate, Fight}
A_entry <- matrix(c(2, -1, 0, 0), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                  dimnames = list(c("Enter", "Stay Out"),
                                  c("Accommodate", "Fight")))
B_entry <- matrix(c(1, -1, 3, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                  dimnames = list(c("Enter", "Stay Out"),
                                  c("Accommodate", "Fight")))

cat("Normal-form representation:\n")#> Normal-form representation:
cat("Entrant payoffs:\n")#> Entrant payoffs:
A_entry#>          Accommodate Fight
#> Enter              2    -1
#> Stay Out           0     0
cat("\nIncumbent payoffs:\n")#> 
#> Incumbent payoffs:
B_entry#>          Accommodate Fight
#> Enter              1    -1
#> Stay Out           3     3
source(here::here("R", "solvers.R"))
ne <- solve_2x2_pure_nash(A_entry, B_entry)
cat("\nPure-strategy Nash equilibria:\n")#> 
#> Pure-strategy Nash equilibria:
for (eq in ne) {
  cat(sprintf("  (%s, %s) -> payoffs (%d, %d)\n",
              rownames(A_entry)[eq[1]], colnames(A_entry)[eq[2]],
              A_entry[eq[1], eq[2]], B_entry[eq[1], eq[2]]))
}#>   (Enter, Accommodate) -> payoffs (2, 1)
#>   (Stay Out, Fight) -> payoffs (0, 3)

heading: 7 Bayesian Games
text: Games incomplete information players hold private types form beliefs opponents. Covers Bayesian Nash equilibrium, signaling games, separating versus pooling equilibria worked job-market signaling example.

heading: Learning objectives
text: Explain types prior beliefs extend normal-form model incomplete information settings.Define Bayesian Nash equilibrium compute small games discrete types.Distinguish signaling games Bayesian games identify separating, pooling, semi-separating equilibria.Implement expected-payoff calculations type uncertainty R produce publication-quality figures.

heading: 7.1 Motivation
text: every game studied far — normal-form games (3) extensive-form games (6) — players share complete knowledge payoff structure. practice, assumption heroic. firm entering new market know rival’s cost structure. job applicant knows ability, employer . bidder auction knows valuation must guess others’ valuations.John Harsanyi’s insight incomplete information can modeled introducing types: player privately observes type drawn commonly known distribution, type determines player’s payoffs. resulting game Bayesian game, appropriate equilibrium concept Bayesian Nash equilibrium (BNE). Osborne (2004, Chapter 9) develops detail, BNE asks player choose strategy — complete plan mapping types actions — optimal expectation players’ types.Signaling games, particularly important class Bayesian games, arise informed player moves first uninformed player observes action responding. Spence’s job-market signaling model classic example: worker’s education choice may reveal (conceal) productivity type employers.

heading: 7.2.1 The Bayesian game model
text: Bayesian game extends standard normal form three additional ingredients:Type spaces. player \(\) type \(\theta_i \\Theta_i\). type encodes private information affects payoffs.Prior beliefs. common prior probability distribution \(p(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n)\) governs joint distribution types. Players update beliefs using Bayes’ rule observing type.Type-contingent payoffs. Player \(\)’s payoff function \(u_i(a_1, \ldots, a_n; \theta_i)\) may depend \(\)’s type (possibly others’ types).strategy Bayesian game function \(s_i : \Theta_i \A_i\) mapping possible type action.

heading: 7.2.2 Bayesian Nash equilibrium
7.2.3 Signaling games
text: signaling game two-player Bayesian game sequential moves:Nature draws sender’s type \(\theta \\Theta\) according prior \(p(\theta)\).sender observes \(\theta\) chooses signal (message) \(m \M\).receiver observes \(m\) (\(\theta\)) chooses action \(\\).Payoffs \(u_S(m, ; \theta)\) \(u_R(m, ; \theta)\) realized.equilibrium concept signaling games perfect Bayesian equilibrium (PBE), requires receiver’s beliefs \(\theta\) derived Bayes’ rule whenever possible.

heading: 7.2.4 Separating vs. pooling equilibria
text: Separating equilibrium: Different types choose different signals, receiver can perfectly infer sender’s type observed message.Pooling equilibrium: types choose signal, receiver learns nothing beyond prior.Semi-separating (partial pooling) equilibrium: types pool others separate, types randomize signals.existence nature equilibria depend cost structure signaling payoff differences across types.

heading: 7.3 Implementation in R
text: implement two-type signaling game inspired Spence job-market model. worker either High-ability (\(\theta_H\)) Low-ability (\(\theta_L\)), equal prior probability. worker chooses whether acquire education (\(E\)) (\(N\)). Education costs differ type: costs High type \(c_H = 1\) Low type \(c_L = 4\). employer offers wage equal expected productivity conditional observed education decision.
code:
# Type parameters
types <- c("High", "Low")
prior <- c(High = 0.5, Low = 0.5)
productivity <- c(High = 6, Low = 2)
edu_cost <- c(High = 1, Low = 4)

# Wages under different employer beliefs
wage_if_high <- productivity["High"]
wage_if_low  <- productivity["Low"]
wage_pooled  <- sum(prior * productivity)

cat(sprintf("Productivity: High = %d, Low = %d\n",
            productivity["High"], productivity["Low"]))#> Productivity: High = 6, Low = 2
cat(sprintf("Education cost: High = %d, Low = %d\n",
            edu_cost["High"], edu_cost["Low"]))#> Education cost: High = 1, Low = 4
cat(sprintf("Pooling wage (prior expectation): %.1f\n", wage_pooled))#> Pooling wage (prior expectation): 4.0

heading: 7.3.1 Expected payoffs under each equilibrium type
code:
# Separating equilibrium: High educates, Low does not
# Employer infers type perfectly from education choice
sep_payoffs <- tibble(
  type = rep(types, each = 2),
  action = rep(c("Educate", "No Education"), times = 2),
  payoff = c(
    wage_if_high - edu_cost["High"],  # High, Educate (separating)
    wage_if_low,                       # High, No Education (deviate)
    wage_if_high - edu_cost["Low"],    # Low, Educate (deviate)
    wage_if_low                        # Low, No Education (separating)
  ),
  equilibrium = c("Separating", "Deviation", "Deviation", "Separating")
)

# Pooling equilibrium: both types educate
# Employer pays pooling wage to educated, low wage to uneducated
pool_payoffs <- tibble(
  type = rep(types, each = 2),
  action = rep(c("Educate", "No Education"), times = 2),
  payoff = c(
    wage_pooled - edu_cost["High"],  # High, Educate (pooling)
    wage_if_low,                      # High, No Education (deviate)
    wage_pooled - edu_cost["Low"],   # Low, Educate (pooling)
    wage_if_low                       # Low, No Education (deviate)
  ),
  equilibrium = c("Pooling", "Deviation", "Pooling", "Deviation")
)

cat("Separating equilibrium payoffs:\n")#> Separating equilibrium payoffs:
sep_payoffs |>
  select(type, action, payoff, equilibrium) |>
  print()#> # A tibble: 4 × 4
#>   type  action       payoff equilibrium
#>   <chr> <chr>         <dbl> <chr>      
#> 1 High  Educate           5 Separating 
#> 2 High  No Education      2 Deviation  
#> 3 Low   Educate           2 Deviation  
#> 4 Low   No Education      2 Separating
cat("\nPooling equilibrium payoffs:\n")#> 
#> Pooling equilibrium payoffs:
pool_payoffs |>
  select(type, action, payoff, equilibrium) |>
  print()#> # A tibble: 4 × 4
#>   type  action       payoff equilibrium
#>   <chr> <chr>         <dbl> <chr>      
#> 1 High  Educate           3 Pooling    
#> 2 High  No Education      2 Deviation  
#> 3 Low   Educate           0 Pooling    
#> 4 Low   No Education      2 Deviation

heading: 7.3.2 Checking incentive compatibility
code:
# Separating equilibrium IC checks
high_sep <- wage_if_high - edu_cost["High"]  # 6 - 1 = 5
high_dev <- wage_if_low                       # 2
low_sep  <- wage_if_low                       # 2
low_dev  <- wage_if_high - edu_cost["Low"]    # 6 - 4 = 2

cat("Separating equilibrium IC checks:\n")#> Separating equilibrium IC checks:
cat(sprintf("  High type: Educate (%.0f) vs No Education (%.0f) -> %s\n",
            high_sep, high_dev,
            ifelse(high_sep >= high_dev, "IC satisfied", "IC violated")))#>   High type: Educate (5) vs No Education (2) -> IC satisfied
cat(sprintf("  Low type:  No Education (%.0f) vs Educate (%.0f) -> %s\n",
            low_sep, low_dev,
            ifelse(low_sep >= low_dev, "IC satisfied", "IC violated")))#>   Low type:  No Education (2) vs Educate (2) -> IC satisfied

heading: 7.3.3 Publication figure: expected payoffs by type and equilibrium
text: Figure 7.1: Expected payoffs worker type separating pooling equilibria job-market signaling game. separating equilibrium High type earns 5 (wage 6 minus education cost 1) Low type earns 2. pooling equilibrium High type earns 3 (pooling wage 4 minus cost 1) Low type earns 0 (pooling wage 4 minus cost 4). Bars shaded equilibrium type.
code:
# Combine equilibrium payoffs for the chosen (non-deviation) actions
eq_summary <- tibble(
  type = rep(types, 2),
  equilibrium = rep(c("Separating", "Pooling"), each = 2),
  payoff = c(
    wage_if_high - edu_cost["High"],  # High, separating
    wage_if_low,                       # Low, separating
    wage_pooled - edu_cost["High"],   # High, pooling
    wage_pooled - edu_cost["Low"]     # Low, pooling
  ),
  action = c("Educate", "No Education", "Educate", "Educate")
)

p_bayesian <- ggplot(eq_summary,
                     aes(x = type, y = payoff, fill = equilibrium)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = payoff),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.7),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Separating" = okabe_ito[1],
                               "Pooling" = okabe_ito[2]),
                    name = "Equilibrium type") +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Expected payoff",
                     limits = c(-1, 6.5),
                     breaks = seq(-1, 6, 1)) +
  scale_x_discrete(name = "Worker type") +
  labs(title = "Payoff Comparison Across Equilibrium Types") +
  theme_publication()

p_bayesian
save_pub_fig(p_bayesian, "bayesian-payoff-comparison")

heading: 7.4 Worked example
text: walk separating equilibrium step step.Step 1: Set game. Nature draws worker’s type: High (\(\theta_H\), productivity 6) Low (\(\theta_L\), productivity 2), probability 0.5. Education costs 1 High type 4 Low type.Step 2: Propose separating strategy. Suppose High type educates Low type .Step 3: Derive employer beliefs. strategy, employer observes \(E\) infers worker High certainty, offering wage 6. Observing \(N\), employer infers worker Low certainty, offering wage 2.Step 4: Check incentive compatibility. High type’s payoff educating \(6 - 1 = 5\), deviating education \(2\). Since \(5 > 2\), High type incentive deviate. Low type’s payoff educating \(2\), deviating education \(6 - 4 = 2\). Since \(2 \geq 2\) (weak IC), Low type strict incentive deviate.Step 5: Verify equilibrium. types play optimally given employer beliefs, beliefs consistent strategies via Bayes’ rule. constitutes perfect Bayesian equilibrium.Note separating equilibrium Pareto-dominates pooling equilibrium High type (payoff 5 vs. 3) weakly better Low type well (payoff 2 vs. 0). distinctive feature signaling models: ability separate benefits high type cost investment (education) socially wasteful enhance actual productivity.
code:
# Payoff summary table
worked_example <- tibble(
  `Worker type` = c("High", "Low"),
  `Equilibrium action` = c("Educate", "No Education"),
  `Wage received` = c(6, 2),
  `Education cost` = c(1, 0),
  `Net payoff` = c(5, 2),
  `Deviation payoff` = c(2, 2),
  `IC satisfied?` = c("Yes (5 > 2)", "Yes (2 >= 2)")
)

worked_example |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Separating Equilibrium in the Job-Market Signaling Game"
  ) |>

heading: 8 Repeated Games
text: repetition transforms strategic interaction. Covers finite infinite horizon repeated games, discount factors, folk theorem, trigger strategies including Grim Trigger Tit--Tat, worked example showing cooperation can sustained infinitely repeated Prisoner’s Dilemma.

heading: Learning objectives
text: Distinguish finitely infinitely repeated games explain role discount factor.State folk theorem characterize set feasible individually rational payoffs.Describe Grim Trigger Tit--Tat strategies derive conditions sustain cooperation.Plot folk-theorem payoff region given stage game R verify trigger-strategy equilibria computationally.

heading: 8.1 Motivation
text: Prisoner’s Dilemma famously bleak prediction: rational players defect, even though mutual cooperation make better . Yet real world, firms competing market quarter quarter often sustain tacit collusion. Nations locked arms races sometimes reach stable agreements. Individuals small communities cooperate routinely without binding contracts.changes game played , repeatedly? Repetition introduces possibility punishment: player defects today can punished tomorrow. future matters enough — , discount factor sufficiently high — threat future punishment can sustain cooperation equilibrium outcome. central message folk theorem, one powerful results game theory.Robert Axelrod’s celebrated computer tournaments (Axelrod, 1981) demonstrated insight empirically: Tit--Tat, simple strategy cooperates initially mirrors opponent’s previous action, emerged tournament winner, outperforming far sophisticated strategies. revisit Axelrod’s tournament 18; lay theoretical foundations.

heading: 8.2.1 The stage game
text: repeated game built stage game \(G = (N, (A_i), (u_i))\) played period \(t = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\) stage game use throughout chapter Prisoner’s Dilemma following payoff matrix:\(T = 5\) temptation payoff, \(R = 3\) reward mutual cooperation, \(P = 1\) punishment mutual defection, \(S = 0\) sucker’s payoff, satisfying standard ordering \(T > R > P > S\).

heading: 8.2.2 Finite vs. infinite horizon
8.2.3 Trigger strategies
text: trigger strategy prescribes cooperation long player deviated, switches permanently (temporarily) punishment phase deviation.Grim Trigger: Cooperate first period. subsequent period, cooperate players cooperated every previous period. single defection triggers permanent mutual defection.Tit--Tat (TFT): Cooperate first period. subsequent period, play whatever opponent played previous period. TFT punishes defection forgives one period — feature proved remarkably effective Axelrod (1981)’s tournament.

heading: 8.2.4 The Grim Trigger condition
8.2.5 The folk theorem
text: Theorem: Folk Theorem (Friedman, 1971)Let \(G\) finite stage game let \(v = (v_1, \ldots, v_n)\) feasible payoff vector strictly dominates player’s minimax payoff \(\underline{v}_i\). sufficiently large \(\delta < 1\), exists subgame-perfect equilibrium infinitely repeated game average payoff \(v\).folk theorem tells us repetition patient players can sustain feasible, individually rational payoff profile — dramatic expansion equilibrium set compared one-shot game. set achievable payoffs forms convex polytope bounded feasibility (convex hull stage-game payoff profiles) individual rationality (player earns least minimax value). See Osborne (2004, Chapter 14) formal proof.

heading: 8.3.1 Stage game setup and Grim Trigger analysis
code:
# Stage game payoffs (Prisoner's Dilemma)
T_payoff <- 5   # temptation
R_payoff <- 3   # reward
P_payoff <- 1   # punishment
S_payoff <- 0   # sucker

# Critical discount factor for Grim Trigger
delta_star <- (T_payoff - R_payoff) / (T_payoff - P_payoff)
cat(sprintf("Critical discount factor (Grim Trigger): delta* = %.4f\n",
            delta_star))#> Critical discount factor (Grim Trigger): delta* = 0.5000

heading: 8.3.2 Payoff comparison across discount factors
code:
# Compare cooperation vs deviation payoffs for a range of delta
delta_seq <- seq(0.01, 0.99, by = 0.01)

payoff_data <- tibble(
  delta = delta_seq,
  cooperate = R_payoff,
  deviate = (1 - delta_seq) * T_payoff + delta_seq * P_payoff
)

payoff_long <- payoff_data |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(cooperate, deviate),
               names_to = "strategy", values_to = "payoff") |>
  mutate(strategy = str_to_title(strategy))

cat("Cooperation payoff (constant):", R_payoff, "\n")#> Cooperation payoff (constant): 3
cat("Deviation payoff at delta = 0.3:",
    round((1 - 0.3) * T_payoff + 0.3 * P_payoff, 2), "\n")#> Deviation payoff at delta = 0.3: 3.8

heading: 8.3.4 Publication figure: folk theorem payoff region
text: Figure 8.1: set achievable payoffs folk theorem Prisoner’s Dilemma. outer polygon (light fill) shows feasible set — convex hull stage-game payoff profiles. shaded interior region represents payoff pairs feasible individually rational (player earns least minimax payoff 1). point shaded region can sustained subgame-perfect equilibrium sufficiently high discount factor.
code:
# Convex hull of feasible payoffs
feasible_hull <- profiles |>
  select(u1, u2) |>
  as.matrix()
hull_idx <- chull(feasible_hull)
hull_points <- feasible_hull[c(hull_idx, hull_idx[1]), ]
hull_df <- as_tibble(hull_points, .name_repair = ~ c("u1", "u2"))

# Individually rational and feasible region
# Intersection of feasible set with u1 >= 1 and u2 >= 1
# The feasible polygon vertices are (3,3), (0,5), (5,0), (1,1)
# Clipping to u1 >= 1 and u2 >= 1 yields vertices:
ir_vertices <- tibble(
  u1 = c(1, 1, 3, 5, 4),
  u2 = c(4, 1, 3, 0, 1)
)

# More precisely, find the individually rational feasible set
# The feasible set edges: (0,5)-(3,3), (3,3)-(5,0), (5,0)-(1,1), (1,1)-(0,5)
# Clipping with u1 >= 1: edge (0,5)-(1,1) at u1=1 gives u2 between 1 and 5
# edge (0,5)-(3,3) at u1=1 gives u2 = 5 - (2/3)*1 = 5 - 2/3 = 13/3
# Clipping with u2 >= 1: edge (5,0)-(1,1) at u2=1 gives u1=1
# edge (5,0)-(3,3) at u2=1 gives u1 = 5 - (5/3)*1 = ... no.
# Let's compute properly.

# Feasible set is convex hull of (3,3), (0,5), (5,0), (1,1)
# The IR region clips this with u1 >= 1, u2 >= 1
# Since (1,1) is already a vertex and all other vertices except (0,5) have u1>=1
# and all except (5,0) have u2>=1, the clipping is:

# Edge from (0,5) to (3,3): parametrically (3t, 5-2t) for t in [0,1]
# u1 = 1 => t = 1/3, u2 = 5 - 2/3 = 13/3
# Edge from (0,5) to (1,1): parametrically (t, 5-4t) for t in [0,1]
# u1 = 1 => t = 1, giving (1,1) -- that's the endpoint
# Edge from (5,0) to (3,3): parametrically (5-2t, 3t) for t in [0,1]
# u2 = 1 => t = 1/3, u1 = 5 - 2/3 = 13/3
# Edge from (5,0) to (1,1): parametrically (5-4t, t) for t in [0,1]
# u2 = 1 => t = 1, giving (1,1)

ir_region <- tibble(
  u1 = c(1, 1, 3, 13/3, 1),
  u2 = c(1, 13/3, 3, 1, 1)
)

# Key labeled points
key_points <- tibble(
  u1 = c(R_payoff, P_payoff, T_payoff, S_payoff),
  u2 = c(R_payoff, P_payoff, S_payoff, T_payoff),
  label = c("(C,C)", "(D,D)", "(D,C)", "(C,D)")
)

p_folk <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = hull_df, aes(x = u1, y = u2),
               fill = "grey90", colour = "grey50",
               linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_polygon(data = ir_region, aes(x = u1, y = u2),
               fill = okabe_ito[2], alpha = 0.3,
               colour = okabe_ito[5], linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = minimax_2, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey40") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = minimax_1, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(data = key_points, aes(x = u1, y = u2),
             size = 3, colour = okabe_ito[6]) +
  geom_text(data = key_points, aes(x = u1, y = u2, label = label),
            vjust = -0.8, size = 3.2, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 2.2, y = 2.5,
           label = "Folk theorem\nachievable set",
           colour = okabe_ito[5], size = 3.5, fontface = "italic") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.3, y = minimax_2 + 0.2,
           label = expression(underline(v)[2]),
           colour = "grey40", size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text", x = minimax_1 + 0.3, y = -0.2,
           label = expression(underline(v)[1]),
           colour = "grey40", size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = expression(u[1]),
                     limits = c(-0.5, 5.8), breaks = 0:5) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = expression(u[2]),
                     limits = c(-0.5, 5.8), breaks = 0:5) +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(title = "Folk Theorem: Feasible and Individually Rational Payoffs") +
  theme_publication()

p_folk
save_pub_fig(p_folk, "folk-theorem-region")

heading: 8.4 Worked example
text: verify step step Grim Trigger sustains cooperation infinitely repeated Prisoner’s Dilemma \(\delta = 0.6\).Step 1: Define stage game. payoffs \(T = 5\), \(R = 3\), \(P = 1\), \(S = 0\) defined .Step 2: Specify strategy. players use Grim Trigger: cooperate initially, continue cooperating long defection occurred. defection, defect forever.Step 3: Compute cooperation payoff. equilibrium path, players cooperate forever. discounted average payoff \(V_C = R = 3\).Step 4: Compute deviation payoff. player defects period \(t\) earns \(T = 5\) period. period \(t + 1\) onward, players defect (Grim Trigger activated), earning \(P = 1\) per period. discounted average payoff deviation :\[V_D = (1 - 0.6) \cdot 5 + 0.6 \cdot 1 = 2.0 + 0.6 = 2.6\]Step 5: Verify equilibrium condition. Since \(V_C = 3.0 > 2.6 = V_D\), deviation unprofitable. Grim Trigger subgame-perfect equilibrium \(\delta = 0.6\).
code:
delta <- 0.6

V_C <- R_payoff
V_D <- (1 - delta) * T_payoff + delta * P_payoff

cat(sprintf("Discount factor: delta = %.1f\n", delta))#> Discount factor: delta = 0.6
cat(sprintf("Cooperation payoff (Grim Trigger): V_C = %.1f\n", V_C))#> Cooperation payoff (Grim Trigger): V_C = 3.0
cat(sprintf("Deviation payoff:                  V_D = %.1f\n", V_D))#> Deviation payoff:                  V_D = 2.6
cat(sprintf("Cooperation sustained? %s (V_C = %.1f %s V_D = %.1f)\n",
            ifelse(V_C >= V_D, "YES", "NO"),
            V_C, ifelse(V_C >= V_D, ">=", "<"), V_D))#> Cooperation sustained? YES (V_C = 3.0 >= V_D = 2.6)
cat(sprintf("\nCritical threshold: delta* = %.4f\n", delta_star))#> 
#> Critical threshold: delta* = 0.5000
cat(sprintf("Current delta = %.1f %s delta* = %.4f\n",
            delta, ifelse(delta >= delta_star, ">=", "<"), delta_star))#> Current delta = 0.6 >= delta* = 0.5000
# Summary table
grim_summary <- tibble(
  Parameter = c("Discount factor (delta)", "Critical threshold (delta*)",
                "Cooperation payoff (V_C)", "Deviation payoff (V_D)",
                "Equilibrium?"),
  Value = c(sprintf("%.1f", delta), sprintf("%.4f", delta_star),
            sprintf("%.1f", V_C), sprintf("%.1f", V_D),
            ifelse(V_C >= V_D, "Yes", "No"))
)

grim_summary |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Grim Trigger Equilibrium Verification",
             subtitle = "Infinitely Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma")

heading: 9.2.1 Characteristic function games
text: TU cooperative game pair \((N, v)\) :\(N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}\) set players.\(v : 2^N \\mathbb{R}\) characteristic function assigning value \(v(S)\) coalition \(S \subseteq N\), \(v(\emptyset) = 0\).value \(v(S)\) represents total payoff members \(S\) can guarantee cooperating, regardless players outside \(S\) . game superadditive \(v(S \cup T) \geq v(S) + v(T)\) disjoint \(S, T \subseteq N\), meaning merging coalitions never destroys value.

heading: 9.2.2 Imputations

9.3.1 Defining a cooperative game

heading: 9.3.2 Computing the Shapley value
9.3.3 Checking the core
code:
# An allocation is in the core if it satisfies all coalition constraints
check_core <- function(x, players, v_func) {
  n <- length(players)
  violations <- character(0)

  # Check all non-empty subsets
  for (k in 1:(2^n - 1)) {
    bits <- as.integer(intToBits(k)[1:n])
    coalition <- players[bits == 1]
    coal_key <- paste(sort(coalition), collapse = "")
    coal_val <- v_func[coal_key]
    alloc_sum <- sum(x[coalition])

    if (alloc_sum < coal_val - 1e-10) {
      violations <- c(violations,
                      sprintf("{%s}: sum = %.2f < v = %.0f",
                              paste(coalition, collapse = ", "),
                              alloc_sum, coal_val))
    }
  }

  list(in_core = length(violations) == 0, violations = violations)
}

# Check if Shapley value is in the core
core_result <- check_core(shapley_vals, players, v)
cat("Is the Shapley value in the core? ",
    ifelse(core_result$in_core, "YES", "NO"), "\n")#> Is the Shapley value in the core?  YES
if (!core_result$in_core) {
  cat("Violations:\n")
  for (viol in core_result$violations) cat("  ", viol, "\n")
}

# Display all coalition constraints
cat("\nCore constraints:\n")#> 
#> Core constraints:
coalitions <- list(
  c("A"), c("B"), c("C"),
  c("A", "B"), c("A", "C"), c("B", "C")
)

for (coal in coalitions) {
  coal_key <- paste(sort(coal), collapse = "")
  cat(sprintf("  x(%s) = %.2f >= v({%s}) = %d : %s\n",
              paste(coal, collapse = " + "),
              sum(shapley_vals[coal]),
              paste(coal, collapse = ", "),
              v[coal_key],
              ifelse(sum(shapley_vals[coal]) >= v[coal_key] - 1e-10,
                     "satisfied", "VIOLATED")))
}#>   x(A) = 20.00 >= v({A}) = 0 : satisfied
#>   x(B) = 20.00 >= v({B}) = 0 : satisfied
#>   x(C) = 20.00 >= v({C}) = 0 : satisfied
#>   x(A + B) = 40.00 >= v({A, B}) = 40 : satisfied
#>   x(A + C) = 40.00 >= v({A, C}) = 40 : satisfied
#>   x(B + C) = 40.00 >= v({B, C}) = 40 : satisfied

text: walk Shapley value calculation municipality water treatment cost-sharing game.Step 1: List permutations. three players, \(3! = 6\) orderings.Step 2: Compute ’s marginal contribution ordering.-B-C: joins empty coalition. \(v(\{\}) - v(\emptyset) = 0 - 0 = 0\).-C-B: . \(MC() = 0\).B--C: joins {B}. \(v(\{,B\}) - v(\{B\}) = 40 - 0 = 40\).C--B: joins {C}. \(v(\{,C\}) - v(\{C\}) = 40 - 0 = 40\).B-C-: joins {B,C}. \(v(\{,B,C\}) - v(\{B,C\}) = 60 - 40 = 20\).C-B-: joins {B,C}. . \(MC() = 20\).Step 3: Average marginal contributions.\[\phi_A = \frac{0 + 0 + 40 + 40 + 20 + 20}{6} = \frac{120}{6} = 20\]Step 4: Verify efficiency. symmetry game (two-player coalitions value), \(\phi_A = \phi_B = \phi_C = 20\). sum \(20 + 20 + 20 = 60 = v(N)\), confirming efficiency.Step 5: Interpret. municipality saves 20 units cooperation. # Verify step by step for player A
mc_A <- c(
  v["A"] - v["0"],     # A first
  v["A"] - v["0"],     # A first (other order)

)

heading: 10.3.2 Benchmarking vectorized vs. loop-based payoff computation
text:
common performance concern game-theoretic computation whether use loops vectorized operations building payoff matrices. benchmark approaches illustrate vectorized code matters — demonstrate produce publication-quality benchmark figure.
Figure 10.1: Computation time building n--n payoff matrix using nested loop versus vectorized outer product. Vectorized operations maintain near-constant time across matrix sizes, loop-based computation scales quadratically. Error bars show plus minus one standard deviation five replications.
code:
# Benchmark: computing a payoff matrix for an n-strategy symmetric game
# Payoff function: u(i,j) = sin(i/n * pi) * cos(j/n * pi) (arbitrary smooth payoffs)

benchmark_payoff <- function(n, method = c("loop", "vectorized")) {

  method <- match.arg(method)

  if (method == "loop") {
    mat <- matrix(0, nrow = n, ncol = n)
    for (i in 1:n) {
      for (j in 1:n) {
        mat[i, j] <- sin(i / n * pi) * cos(j / n * pi)
      }
    }
  } else {
    rows <- seq_len(n)
    cols <- seq_len(n)
    mat <- outer(sin(rows / n * pi), cos(cols / n * pi))
  }
  mat
}

# Run benchmarks for various matrix sizes
sizes <- c(10, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000)
n_reps <- 5

results <- map_dfr(sizes, function(n) {
  loop_times <- replicate(n_reps, {
    start <- proc.time()["elapsed"]
    benchmark_payoff(n, "loop")
    proc.time()["elapsed"] - start
  })

  vec_times <- replicate(n_reps, {
    start <- proc.time()["elapsed"]
    benchmark_payoff(n, "vectorized")
    proc.time()["elapsed"] - start
  })

  tibble(
    n = n,
    method = rep(c("Loop", "Vectorized"), each = n_reps),
    time_sec = c(loop_times, vec_times)
  )
})

# Summarize
bench_summary <- results |>
  group_by(n, method) |>
  summarise(
    mean_time = mean(time_sec),
    sd_time = sd(time_sec),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
p_bench <- ggplot(bench_summary,
                  aes(x = n, y = mean_time, colour = method, shape = method)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = pmax(0, mean_time - sd_time),
                    ymax = mean_time + sd_time),
                width = 0.05, linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = okabe_ito[c(1, 3)],
                      name = "Method") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(16, 17), name = "Method") +
  scale_x_log10(name = "Matrix dimension (n)",
                breaks = sizes,
                labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_log10(name = "Time (seconds)",
                labels = scales::label_number(accuracy = 0.0001)) +
  labs(title = "Payoff Matrix Construction: Loop vs. Vectorized") +
  theme_publication() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p_bench

heading: 11.2.1 Cooperative games and characteristic functions
text: transferable utility (TU) cooperative game pair \((N, v)\) \(N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}\) set players \(v : 2^N \\mathbb{R}\) characteristic function satisfying \(v(\emptyset) = 0\). value \(v(S)\) represents total payoff coalition \(S \subseteq N\) can guarantee .Definition: Weighted Voting GameA weighted voting game \([q; w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n]\) quota \(q\) player weights \(w_i\). coalition \(S\) winning \(\sum_{\S} w_i \geq q\), giving \(v(S) = 1\); otherwise \(v(S) = 0\).

heading: 11.2.2 The Shapley value
11.2.3 The Banzhaf power index
11.2.4 The GameTheory package API
text: GameTheory package provides functions including:DefineGame() — create cooperative game characteristic function vector.ShapleyValue() — compute Shapley value TU game.Nucleolus() — find nucleolus (allocation minimizing maximum dissatisfaction).Core() — compute check non-emptiness core.BanzhafValue() — compute Banzhaf power index.Since package installed, now implement concepts scratch.

heading: 11.3.1 Defining a characteristic function
text: represent characteristic function named numeric vector indexed coalition labels. \(n\) players, \(2^n - 1\) non-empty coalitions.
code:
# Build characteristic function for a weighted voting game
weighted_voting_game <- function(quota, weights) {
  n <- length(weights)
  players <- seq_len(n)
  # Enumerate all 2^n subsets (including empty set)
  v <- numeric(2^n)
  coalition_labels <- character(2^n)
  for (k in 0:(2^n - 1)) {
    members <- players[as.logical(intToBits(k)[1:n])]
    coalition_labels[k + 1] <- paste(members, collapse = ",")
    if (length(members) > 0 && sum(weights[members]) >= quota) {
      v[k + 1] <- 1
    }
  }
  names(v) <- coalition_labels
  v
}

# Three-player weighted voting game: Player 1 has veto power
weights <- c(3, 1, 1)
quota <- 4
v <- weighted_voting_game(quota, weights)
cat("Characteristic function [4; 3, 1, 1]:\n")#> Characteristic function [4; 3, 1, 1]:
v[v > 0 | names(v) == ""]#>         1,2   1,3 1,2,3 
#>     0     1     1     1

heading: 11.3.2 Computing the Shapley value via permutations
11.3.3 Shapley value via the analytical formula
code:
# Shapley value using the combinatorial formula from \@ref(eq:shapley-value)
shapley_value_formula <- function(v, n) {
  players <- seq_len(n)
  phi <- numeric(n)

  for (i in players) {
    others <- setdiff(players, i)
    # Enumerate all subsets of others
    for (k in 0:(2^(n - 1) - 1)) {
      members <- others[as.logical(intToBits(k)[1:(n - 1)])]
      s <- length(members)
      weight <- factorial(s) * factorial(n - s - 1) / factorial(n)
      # v(S union {i}) - v(S)
      key_with <- paste(sort(c(members, i)), collapse = ",")
      key_without <- if (length(members) == 0) "" else
        paste(sort(members), collapse = ",")
      marginal <- v[key_with] - ifelse(key_without == "", 0, v[key_without])
      phi[i] <- phi[i] + weight * marginal
    }
  }
  phi
}

shapley_formula <- shapley_value_formula(v, 3)
names(shapley_formula) <- paste("Player", 1:3)
cat("Shapley values (analytical formula):\n")#> Shapley values (analytical formula):
print(shapley_formula)#> Player 1 Player 2 Player 3 
#>    0.667    0.167    0.167
cat("\nMaximum difference from permutation method:",
    max(abs(shapley - shapley_formula)), "\n")#> 
#> Maximum difference from permutation method: 0

heading: 11.3.4 Computing the Banzhaf power index
code:
banzhaf_index <- function(v, n) {
  players <- seq_len(n)
  beta <- numeric(n)

  for (i in players) {
    others <- setdiff(players, i)
    swings <- 0
    for (k in 0:(2^(n - 1) - 1)) {
      members <- others[as.logical(intToBits(k)[1:(n - 1)])]
      key_with <- paste(sort(c(members, i)), collapse = ",")
      key_without <- if (length(members) == 0) "" else
        paste(sort(members), collapse = ",")
      marginal <- v[key_with] - ifelse(key_without == "", 0, v[key_without])
      swings <- swings + marginal
    }
    beta[i] <- swings / 2^(n - 1)
  }
  beta
}

banzhaf <- banzhaf_index(v, 3)
names(banzhaf) <- paste("Player", 1:3)
cat("Raw Banzhaf indices for [4; 3, 1, 1]:\n")#> Raw Banzhaf indices for [4; 3, 1, 1]:
print(banzhaf)#> Player 1 Player 2 Player 3 
#>     0.75     0.25     0.25
cat("\nNormalized Banzhaf indices:\n")#> 
#> Normalized Banzhaf indices:
print(banzhaf / sum(banzhaf))#> Player 1 Player 2 Player 3 
#>      0.6      0.2      0.2

heading: 11.3.5 Comparing power indices across coalition structures
text:
Figure 11.1: Shapley values normalized Banzhaf indices three different weighted voting games three players. veto player (Player 1) commands disproportionate power relative weight share.
11.1 shows, game symmetric players equal power. Player 1’s weight advantage grows, Shapley value Banzhaf index assign disproportionately larger share power — exceeding raw weight share. strong veto game \([4; 3, 1, 1]\), Player 1 essential every winning coalition, indices capture clearly.
code:
games <- list(
  "Symmetric\n[2; 1,1,1]"  = list(quota = 2, weights = c(1, 1, 1)),
  "Moderate veto\n[3; 2,1,1]" = list(quota = 3, weights = c(2, 1, 1)),
  "Strong veto\n[4; 3,1,1]"  = list(quota = 4, weights = c(3, 1, 1))
)

results <- map_dfr(names(games), function(game_name) {
  g <- games[[game_name]]
  v_game <- weighted_voting_game(g$quota, g$weights)
  shap <- shapley_value_formula(v_game, 3)
  banz <- banzhaf_index(v_game, 3)
  banz_norm <- banz / sum(banz)

  tibble(
    game = game_name,
    player = rep(paste("Player", 1:3), 2),
    index = rep(c("Shapley value", "Banzhaf (normalized)"), each = 3),
    value = c(shap, banz_norm)
  )
})

p_comparison <- ggplot(results,
                       aes(x = player, y = value, fill = index)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ game, nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(okabe_ito[1], okabe_ito[2])) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  theme_publication() +
  labs(
    title = "Power Indices Across Weighted Voting Games",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Share of total power",
    fill = "Index"
  )
p_comparison
save_pub_fig(p_comparison, "shapley-comparison", width = 7, height = 5)

perms <- all_permutations(3)

for (r in seq_len(nrow(perms))) {
  perm <- perms[r, ]
  countries <- c("Germany", "France", "Belgium")[perm]
  pos_belgium <- which(perm == 3)
  predecessors <- if (pos_belgium > 1) perm[1:(pos_belgium - 1)] else integer(0)

    w_before <- sum(eu_weights[predecessors])

  cat(sprintf("  %s -> %s -> %s | Before Belgium: weight=%d, After: %d | %s\n",
              countries[1], countries[2], countries[3],
              w_before, w_after,
              ifelse(pivotal, "PIVOTAL", "")))
}#>   Belgium -> France -> Germany | Before Belgium: weight=0, After: 1 | 
#>   France -> Belgium -> Germany | Before Belgium: weight=3, After: 4 |

#>   Germany -> Belgium -> France | Before Belgium: weight=4, After: 5 | PIVOTAL
#>   Germany -> France -> Belgium | Before Belgium: weight=7, After: 8 |
cat(sprintf("\nShapley value for Belgium: %d/6 = %.4f\n",
            round(eu_shapley[3] * 6), eu_shapley[3]))#>

heading: 12.3.1 Coalition encoding and game definition
code:
# Convert player set to coalition index (binary encoding)
coalition_idx <- function(members, n) sum(2^(members - 1))

# Convert index back to player set
idx_to_players <- function(idx, n) which(as.logical(intToBits(idx)[1:n]))

# Generate all non-empty coalitions
all_coalitions <- function(n) {
  lapply(1:(2^n - 1), function(k) idx_to_players(k, n))
}

# Three-player production game
# A (materials), B (factory), C (distribution)
# Index: 1={A}, 2={B}, 3={A,B}, 4={C}, 5={A,C}, 6={B,C}, 7={A,B,C}
v_prod <- c(10, 20, 50, 15, 40, 45, 80)
names(v_prod) <- c("{A}", "{B}", "{A,B}", "{C}", "{A,C}", "{B,C}", "{A,B,C}")

cat("Production game characteristic function:\n")#> Production game characteristic function:

#>      10      20      50      15      40      45      80

heading: 12.3.2 Computing the core via linear constraints
text: three players efficiency \(x_A + x_B + x_C = 80\), substituting \(x_C = 80 - x_A - x_B\) reduces core 2D feasibility problem:
code:
# After substitution, the six constraints are:
#   x_A >= 10,  x_B >= 20,  x_A + x_B <= 65 (from x_C >= 15)
#   x_A + x_B >= 50,  x_B <= 40 (from x_A + x_C >= 40),  x_A <= 35

core_constraints <- tribble(
  ~constraint,         ~description,
  "x_A >= 10",        "A individual rationality",
  "x_B >= 20",        "B individual rationality",
  "x_A + x_B <= 65",  "C individual rationality",
  "x_A + x_B >= 50",  "AB coalition rationality",
  "x_B <= 40",        "AC coalition rationality",
  "x_A <= 35",        "BC coalition rationality"
)
core_constraints |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Core Constraints (after efficiency substitution)") |>

heading: 12.3.3 Finding core vertices
code:
# Find vertices by intersecting all pairs of boundary lines
boundary_intersections <- function() {
  bounds <- list(c(1,0,10), c(0,1,20), c(1,1,65),
                 c(1,1,50), c(0,1,40), c(1,0,35))
  vertices <- tibble(x_A = numeric(), x_B = numeric(), x_C = numeric())

  for (i in 1:(length(bounds) - 1)) {
    for (j in (i + 1):length(bounds)) {
      A_mat <- matrix(c(bounds[[i]][1:2], bounds[[j]][1:2]),
                      nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
      b_vec <- c(bounds[[i]][3], bounds[[j]][3])
      if (abs(det(A_mat)) < 1e-10) next
      sol <- solve(A_mat, b_vec)
      xa <- sol[1]; xb <- sol[2]; xc <- 80 - xa - xb

      feasible <- (xa >= 10 - 1e-10) && (xb >= 20 - 1e-10) &&
        (xa + xb <= 65 + 1e-10) && (xa + xb >= 50 - 1e-10) &&
        (xb <= 40 + 1e-10) && (xa <= 35 + 1e-10)

      if (feasible) {
        vertices <- bind_rows(vertices,
          tibble(x_A = round(xa, 2), x_B = round(xb, 2), x_C = round(xc, 2)))
      }
    }
  }
  distinct(vertices)
}

core_verts <- boundary_intersections()
core_verts |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Vertices of the Core") |>
  cols_align(align = "center")

heading: 12.3.4 Computing the nucleolus
code:
# Compute excesses for all proper coalitions
compute_excesses <- function(x, v_vec, n) {
  coalitions <- all_coalitions(n)
  grand_idx <- 2^n - 1
  exc <- numeric(0); nms <- character(0)
  for (k in seq_along(coalitions)) {
    if (k == grand_idx) next
    S <- coalitions[[k]]
    exc <- c(exc, v_vec[k] - sum(x[S]))
    nms <- c(nms, paste(S, collapse = ","))
  }
  names(exc) <- nms
  exc
}

# Nucleolus via grid search (tractable for 3 players)
find_nucleolus <- function(v_vec, n, grid_size = 300) {
  grand_val <- v_vec[2^n - 1]
  best_x <- NULL; best_theta <- Inf
  xa_seq <- seq(10, 35, length.out = grid_size)
  xb_seq <- seq(20, 40, length.out = grid_size)

  for (xa in xa_seq) {
    for (xb in xb_seq) {
      xc <- grand_val - xa - xb
      if (xc < 15 - 1e-10 || xa + xb < 50 - 1e-10 ||
          xa + xb > 65 + 1e-10) next
      max_exc <- max(compute_excesses(c(xa, xb, xc), v_vec, n))
      if (max_exc < best_theta) {
        best_theta <- max_exc; best_x <- c(xa, xb, xc)
      }
    }
  }
  names(best_x) <- c("A", "B", "C")
  best_x
}

nuc <- find_nucleolus(v_prod, 3)
cat("Nucleolus (approximate):\n")#> Nucleolus (approximate):
cat(sprintf("  A: %.2f, B: %.2f, C: %.2f (sum: %.2f)\n",
            nuc[1], nuc[2], nuc[3], sum(nuc)))#>   A: 25.05, B: 32.44, C: 22.51 (sum: 80.00)
cat("\nExcesses at the nucleolus:\n")#> 
#> Excesses at the nucleolus:
print(round(sort(compute_excesses(nuc, v_prod, 3), decreasing = TRUE), 2))#>    1,2      3    1,3    2,3      2      1 
#>  -7.49  -7.51  -7.56  -9.95 -12.44 -15.05
