code:
# Shapley value via permutation enumeration
shapley_prod <- function() {
  perms <- list(c(1,2,3), c(1,3,2), c(2,1,3),
                c(2,3,1), c(3,1,2), c(3,2,1))
  phi <- c(0, 0, 0)
  get_v <- function(m) {
    if (length(m) == 0) return(0)
    v_prod[coalition_idx(m, 3)]
  }
  for (perm in perms) {
    coal <- integer(0)
    for (p in perm) {
      v_before <- get_v(coal)
      coal <- c(coal, p)
      phi[p] <- phi[p] + (get_v(sort(coal)) - v_before)
    }
  }
  phi / length(perms)
}
shap <- shapley_prod()

# Barycentric to Cartesian simplex coordinates
to_simplex <- function(xA, xB, xC) {
  total <- xA + xB + xC
  a <- xA / total; b <- xB / total; cc <- xC / total
  tibble(x = b + cc / 2, y = cc * sqrt(3) / 2)
}

simplex_verts <- tibble(
  label = c("A gets 80", "B gets 80", "C gets 80"),
  x = c(0, 1, 0.5), y = c(0, 0, sqrt(3) / 2)
)

core_simplex <- map_dfr(seq_len(nrow(core_verts)), function(i) {
  to_simplex(core_verts$x_A[i], core_verts$x_B[i], core_verts$x_C[i])
})
hull_order <- chull(core_simplex$x, core_simplex$y)
core_hull <- core_simplex[c(hull_order, hull_order[1]), ]

shap_pt <- to_simplex(shap[1], shap[2], shap[3])
nuc_pt <- to_simplex(nuc[1], nuc[2], nuc[3])

p_simplex <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = simplex_verts, aes(x = x, y = y),
               fill = "grey95", colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_polygon(data = core_hull, aes(x = x, y = y),
               fill = okabe_ito[3], alpha = 0.3,
               colour = okabe_ito[3], linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(data = core_simplex, aes(x = x, y = y),
             colour = okabe_ito[3], size = 2) +
  geom_point(data = shap_pt, aes(x = x, y = y),
             colour = okabe_ito[1], size = 4, shape = 17) +
  annotate("text", x = shap_pt$x + 0.04, y = shap_pt$y + 0.02,
           label = sprintf("Shapley\n(%.1f, %.1f, %.1f)",
                           shap[1], shap[2], shap[3]),
           colour = okabe_ito[1], size = 3, fontface = "bold", hjust = 0) +
  geom_point(data = nuc_pt, aes(x = x, y = y),
             colour = okabe_ito[5], size = 4, shape = 15) +

           label = sprintf("Nucleolus\n(%.1f, %.1f, %.1f)",

        axis.ticks = element_blank(), panel.grid = element_blank()) +

Equal division (\(\approx 26.67\) ) fails \(x_B + x_C \approx 53.33 < 45\) satisfied, Firm B 26.67 prefer partner C 45 — coalition rationality constraint \(x_A + x_B \geq 50\) violated. illustrates simplistic “fair” divisions often unstable.Firm B receives largest share solution concepts, reflecting central role two valuable pair coalitions (\(v(\{,B\}) = 50\) \(v(\{B,C\}) = 45\)). Firm , despite lowest stand-alone value, earns well 10 generates substantial synergies B.
code:
check_core <- function(x, v_vec, n, labels) {
  grand <- v_vec[2^n - 1]
  coalitions <- all_coalitions(n)
  all_pass <- abs(sum(x) - grand) < 0.01
  cat(sprintf("Efficiency: sum = %.2f -- %s\n",
              sum(x), ifelse(all_pass, "PASS", "FAIL")))
  for (k in 1:(2^n - 2)) {
    S <- coalitions[[k]]
    pass <- sum(x[S]) >= v_vec[k] - 0.01
    if (!pass) all_pass <- FALSE
    cat(sprintf("  v(%s) = %d, x(%s) = %.2f -- %s\n",
                paste(labels[S], collapse = ","), v_vec[k],
                paste(labels[S], collapse = ","), sum(x[S]),
                ifelse(pass, "PASS", "FAIL")))
  }
  cat(sprintf("In core: %s\n\n", ifelse(all_pass, "YES", "NO")))
}

labels <- c("A", "B", "C")
equal <- rep(80 / 3, 3)

cat("=== Shapley value ===\n")#> === Shapley value ===
check_core(shap, v_prod, 3, labels)#> Efficiency: sum = 80.00 -- PASS
#>   v(A) = 10, x(A) = 24.17 -- PASS
#>   v(B) = 20, x(B) = 31.67 -- PASS
#>   v(A,B) = 50, x(A,B) = 55.83 -- PASS
#>   v(C) = 15, x(C) = 24.17 -- PASS

#>   v(A) = 10, x(A) = 25.05 -- PASS

heading: 13.2.1 The gtree DSL
text: gtree package models extensive-form game sequence stages. stage specifies:moves: named player “nature” (chance nodes).actions available: possibly conditional variables set earlier stages.payoffs result: formulas reference earlier actions.typical gtree specification looks like (pseudocode, since gtree installed):key design insight condition clauses allow stages contingent prior choices, analyst describes game linearly even though underlying tree branches. package internally expands specification full game tree correctly linked information sets.
code:
game <- new_game(
  players = c("Proposer", "Responder"),
  stages = list(
    stage("offer",
      player = "Proposer",
      actions = list(action("split", c("Fair", "Unfair")))
    ),
    stage("response",
      player = "Responder",
      actions = list(action("answer", c("Accept", "Reject")))
    )
  ),
  payoffs = list(
    payoff("Proposer",   ~ case_when(
      answer == "Reject" ~ 0,
      split == "Fair"    ~ 5,
      TRUE               ~ 8)),
    payoff("Responder",  ~ case_when(
      answer == "Reject" ~ 0,
      split == "Fair"    ~ 5,
      TRUE               ~ 2))
  )
)

heading: 13.2.2 Gambit integration
text: game defined, gtree can export Gambit’s .efg (extensive-form game) format invoke Gambit’s command-line solvers:gambit-enumpure enumerates pure-strategy Nash equilibria.gambit-enummixed enumerates mixed-strategy equilibria two-player games.gambit-lcp uses Lemke–Howson algorithm bimatrix games (see 15).gambit-logit computes quantal response equilibria.makes gtree convenient R front-end industrial-strength solvers without requiring analyst learn Gambit’s file format command-line interface.

heading: 13.2.3 Backward induction for perfect-information games
13.2.4 The ultimatum game
text: ultimatum game canonical example behavioral economics. Proposer sum money (say, 10 units) offers split Responder. Responder either Accepts (receive shares) Rejects (receive nothing). Despite intuition positive offer accepted, experimental evidence consistently shows offers 20–30% frequently rejected.game-theoretic prediction backward induction stark: Responder accept positive offer (since something better nothing), therefore Proposer offer minimum possible amount. divergence theory behavior makes ultimatum game rich pedagogical example.

heading: 13.3.1 A flexible game tree representation
text: represent game trees nested lists. node either decision node (player children) terminal node (payoffs players).

heading: 13.3.2 Building the ultimatum game tree
text: predicted theory, backward induction selects Unfair offer (since 8 > 5 Proposer) Responder Accepts (since 2 > 0). SPE payoffs (8, 2).
code:
# Ultimatum game: Proposer offers Fair (5,5) or Unfair (8,2)
# Responder Accepts or Rejects each offer

ultimatum_game <- make_decision(1, list(
  "Fair (5,5)" = make_decision(2, list(
    "Accept" = make_terminal(c(5, 5)),
    "Reject" = make_terminal(c(0, 0))
  )),
  "Unfair (8,2)" = make_decision(2, list(
    "Accept" = make_terminal(c(8, 2)),
    "Reject" = make_terminal(c(0, 0))
  ))
))

result <- solve_by_backward_induction(ultimatum_game)
cat("Subgame-perfect equilibrium payoffs:", result$payoffs, "\n\n")#> Subgame-perfect equilibrium payoffs: 8 2
cat("SPE path:\n")#> SPE path:
for (step in result$path) {
  player_label <- c("Proposer", "Responder")[step$player]
  cat(glue("  {player_label} chooses: {step$action}"), "\n")
}#>   Proposer chooses: Unfair (8,2) 
#>   Responder chooses: Accept

heading: 13.3.3 Visualizing the game tree
text:
build publication-quality game tree figure, first flatten tree data frame nodes edges, use ggplot2 render equilibrium path highlighted.
Figure 13.1: Game tree ultimatum game. Proposer (orange node) chooses Fair Unfair split; Responder (blue nodes) Accepts Rejects. bold orange path marks subgame-perfect equilibrium: Proposer offers Unfair (8, 2) Responder Accepts.
figure makes structure SPE transparent. Responder’s decision nodes, Accepting yields positive payoff Rejecting yields zero — Responder always Accepts. Knowing , Proposer selects split maximizes share, choosing Unfair offer.
code:
# Flatten tree into node and edge data frames for plotting
flatten_tree <- function(node, node_id = 1, depth = 0, x_center = 0,
                         x_spread = 2, eq_path = NULL) {
  nodes <- tibble()
  edges <- tibble()

  if (node$type == "terminal") {
    payoff_label <- paste0("(", paste(node$payoffs, collapse = ", "), ")")
    nodes <- tibble(
      id = node_id, depth = depth, x = x_center,
      label = payoff_label, node_type = "terminal", player = NA_integer_
    )
    return(list(nodes = nodes, edges = edges, next_id = node_id + 1))
  }

  # Decision node
  player_labels <- c("Proposer", "Responder")
  nodes <- tibble(
    id = node_id, depth = depth, x = x_center,
    label = player_labels[node$player], node_type = "decision",
    player = node$player
  )

  n_children <- length(node$children)
  child_positions <- seq(-x_spread / 2, x_spread / 2, length.out = n_children)
  next_id <- node_id + 1

  for (i in seq_along(node$children)) {
    action_name <- names(node$children)[i]
    child_node <- node$children[[i]]

    # Check if this edge is on the equilibrium path
    on_eq_path <- FALSE
    if (!is.null(eq_path) && length(eq_path) > 0) {
      step <- eq_path[[1]]
      if (step$player == node$player && step$action == action_name) {
        on_eq_path <- TRUE
      }
    }

    child_result <- flatten_tree(
      child_node, next_id, depth + 1,
      x_center + child_positions[i], x_spread / 2,
      eq_path = if (on_eq_path && length(eq_path) > 1) eq_path[-1] else
                if (on_eq_path) list() else NULL
    )

    new_edge <- tibble(
      from_id = node_id, to_id = next_id,
      from_x = x_center, from_y = -depth,
      to_x = x_center + child_positions[i], to_y = -(depth + 1),
      action = action_name, on_eq_path = on_eq_path
    )

    edges <- bind_rows(edges, new_edge, child_result$edges)
    nodes <- bind_rows(nodes, child_result$nodes)
    next_id <- child_result$next_id
  }

  list(nodes = nodes, edges = edges, next_id = next_id)
}
tree_data <- flatten_tree(
  ultimatum_game, eq_path = result$path, x_spread = 4
)

nodes_df <- tree_data$nodes |>
  mutate(y = -depth)

edges_df <- tree_data$edges

p_tree <- ggplot() +
  # Draw edges (non-equilibrium first, then equilibrium on top)
  geom_segment(
    data = edges_df |> filter(!on_eq_path),
    aes(x = from_x, y = from_y, xend = to_x, yend = to_y),
    colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    data = edges_df |> filter(on_eq_path),
    aes(x = from_x, y = from_y, xend = to_x, yend = to_y),
    colour = okabe_ito[1], linewidth = 1.5
  ) +
  # Action labels on edges
  geom_label(
    data = edges_df,
    aes(x = (from_x + to_x) / 2, y = (from_y + to_y) / 2, label = action),
    size = 2.8, label.size = 0, fill = "white", alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  # Decision nodes
  geom_point(
    data = nodes_df |> filter(node_type == "decision"),
    aes(x = x, y = y, colour = factor(player)),
    size = 8
  ) +
  # Decision node labels
  geom_text(
    data = nodes_df |> filter(node_type == "decision"),
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
    size = 2.5, colour = "white", fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  # Terminal node labels (payoffs)
  geom_label(
    data = nodes_df |> filter(node_type == "terminal"),
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
    size = 3, fill = "grey95", label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines")
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("1" = okabe_ito[1], "2" = okabe_ito[2]),
    labels = c("Proposer", "Responder"),
    name = "Player"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    xlim = c(-3, 3),
    ylim = c(-2.5, 0.3)
  ) +
  labs(title = "Ultimatum Game: Subgame-Perfect Equilibrium Path") +
  theme_publication() +
  theme(
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )

p_tree
save_pub_fig(p_tree, "ultimatum-game-tree", width = 8, height = 5.5)

heading: 13.4 Worked example
text: Let us extend ultimatum game allow three possible offers solve step step.Setup. Proposer 10 units can offer three splits:Responder observes offer chooses Accept Reject. Rejected, receive 0.Step--step reasoning:Responder’s subgames. three decision nodes, Responder compares Accept vs. Reject. Since Accepting always yields positive payoff (6, 5, 1) Rejecting yields 0, Responder Accepts every offer.Responder’s subgames. three decision nodes, Responder compares Accept vs. Reject. Since Accepting always yields positive payoff (6, 5, 1) Rejecting yields 0, Responder Accepts every offer.Proposer’s choice. Knowing Responder Accept offer, Proposer compares payoffs: 4 (Generous), 5 (Fair), 9 (Greedy). Proposer selects Greedy offer.Proposer’s choice. Knowing Responder Accept offer, Proposer compares payoffs: 4 (Generous), 5 (Fair), 9 (Greedy). Proposer selects Greedy offer.SPE outcome. Proposer offers (9, 1) Responder Accepts, yielding equilibrium payoffs \((9, 1)\).SPE outcome. Proposer offers (9, 1) Responder Accepts, yielding equilibrium payoffs \((9, 1)\).result highlights starkness game-theoretic prediction: rational, self-interested players reach extreme outcome. large experimental literature ultimatum games shows prediction fails descriptively — Responders frequently reject low offers Proposers often offer 40–50% — motivates models incorporating fairness preferences, spite, social norms.
code:
# Three-offer ultimatum game
ultimatum_3 <- make_decision(1, list(
  "Generous (4,6)" = make_decision(2, list(
    "Accept" = make_terminal(c(4, 6)),
    "Reject" = make_terminal(c(0, 0))
  )),
  "Fair (5,5)" = make_decision(2, list(
    "Accept" = make_terminal(c(5, 5)),
    "Reject" = make_terminal(c(0, 0))
  )),
  "Greedy (9,1)" = make_decision(2, list(
    "Accept" = make_terminal(c(9, 1)),
    "Reject" = make_terminal(c(0, 0))
  ))
))

result_3 <- solve_by_backward_induction(ultimatum_3)
cat("SPE payoffs:", result_3$payoffs, "\n\n")#> SPE payoffs: 9 1
cat("SPE path:\n")#> SPE path:
for (step in result_3$path) {
  player_label <- c("Proposer", "Responder")[step$player]
  cat(glue("  {player_label} chooses: {step$action}"), "\n")
}#>   Proposer chooses: Greedy (9,1) 
#>   Responder chooses: Accept

heading: 13.4.1 Scaling analysis
text:
backward induction perform game tree grows? generate random trees varying depth branching factor record solution times.
Figure 13.2: Backward induction solution time function number terminal nodes. binary ternary trees show approximately linear growth, confirming O(n) complexity algorithm.
log-log plot confirms expected linear relationship tree size computation time. Binary trees depth 8 \(2^8 = 256\) terminal nodes solve well millisecond. Even ternary trees depth 8 (\(3^8 = 6{,}561\) terminals) remain fast. real computational challenge arises imperfect information, backward induction longer applies one must resort solvers like Gambit.
code:
# Generate random perfect-information game trees
generate_random_tree <- function(depth, branching, n_players = 2) {
  if (depth == 0) {
    return(make_terminal(runif(n_players, -5, 5)))
  }
  player <- ((depth - 1) %% n_players) + 1
  children <- setNames(
    lapply(seq_len(branching), function(i) {
      generate_random_tree(depth - 1, branching, n_players)
    }),
    paste0("A", seq_len(branching))
  )
  make_decision(player, children)
}

configs <- expand.grid(depth = 2:8, branching = c(2, 3)) |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(n_terminals = branching^depth, time_ms = NA_real_)

for (i in seq_len(nrow(configs))) {
  tree <- generate_random_tree(configs$depth[i], configs$branching[i])
  elapsed <- system.time(solve_by_backward_induction(tree))["elapsed"]
  configs$time_ms[i] <- elapsed * 1000
}

configs |>
  mutate(branching = factor(branching)) |>
  select(depth, branching, n_terminals, time_ms) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Backward Induction: Solution Time by Tree Size") |>
  fmt_number(columns = time_ms, decimals = 2) |>
  fmt_number(columns = n_terminals, use_seps = TRUE, decimals = 0) |>
  cols_label(
    depth = "Depth", branching = "Branching",
    n_terminals = "Terminal Nodes", time_ms = "Time (ms)"
  )
p_scaling <- configs |>
  mutate(branching = factor(branching,
    labels = c("Binary (b=2)", "Ternary (b=3)")
  )) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n_terminals, y = time_ms,
             colour = branching, shape = branching)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = okabe_ito[c(1, 2)]) +
  scale_x_log10(name = "Number of terminal nodes",
                labels = label_comma()) +
  scale_y_log10(name = "Solution time (ms)") +
  labs(
    title = "Backward Induction Scales Linearly with Tree Size",
    colour = "Tree type", shape = "Tree type"
  ) +
  theme_publication()

p_scaling
save_pub_fig(p_scaling, "gtree-scaling-analysis", width = 7, height = 4.5)

heading: 14 nashpy via reticulate
text: Accessing Python’s nashpy library R via reticulate Nash equilibrium computation — conceptual workflow, pure-R support enumeration equivalent alternative, comparative analysis across canonical games.

heading: Learning objectives
text: Describe reticulate workflow calling Python libraries R explain cross-language interop useful game theory.Understand nashpy’s support enumeration algorithm finds Nash equilibria bimatrix game.Implement pure-R support enumeration solver general two-player games.Apply solver canonical games (Prisoner’s Dilemma, Battle Sexes, Matching Pennies) compare equilibrium structures.Recognize computational limits support enumeration alternative algorithms needed.

heading: 14.1 Motivation
text: R’s ecosystem non-cooperative game theory modest. solvers built 4 handle 2x2 games, many interesting strategic interactions larger: 3x3 Rock-Paper-Scissors variant, 4x4 coordination game, asymmetric contest different numbers strategies per player. cases, need general-purpose solvers.Python’s nashpy library, developed Vince Knight, provides exactly . implements support enumeration, vertex enumeration, Lemke–Howson algorithm two-player games arbitrary size. reticulate package lets R users call Python seamlessly, passing matrices back forth collecting results R objects.However, depending reticulate correctly configured Python environment introduces fragility. Python version conflicts, virtual environment management, package installation issues can derail workflow. reproducible research teaching, pure-R fallback valuable. chapter describes approaches: show reticulate + nashpy workflow looks like readers can adopt Python solvers genuinely needed, implement pure-R support enumeration solver produces results. apply several canonical games visualize equilibrium structure varies across different strategic settings.

heading: 14.2.1 The reticulate bridge
text: reticulate package converts R objects Python objects vice versa. typical workflow calling nashpy R four steps.Step 1: Configure Python environment.install_miniconda() call downloads installs minimal Python distribution. py_install() call installs nashpy (dependency NumPy) environment. needs happen per machine.Step 2: Import nashpy create game.import() call loads Python module R’s session. R matrices automatically converted NumPy arrays passed Python functions.Step 3: Compute equilibria.support_enumeration() method returns Python generator. Reticulate’s iterate() converts R list equilibria, equilibrium pair probability vectors.Step 4: Collect results.use nashpy via reticulateThe Python bridge valuable need algorithms beyond simple support enumeration — vertex enumeration degenerate games, Lemke–Howson algorithm games larger 2x2, integration Python scientific computing tools. standard 2x2 analysis, pure-R approach simpler external dependencies.
code:
library(reticulate)
install_miniconda()          # one-time setup
py_install("nashpy")         # install nashpy into the conda environmentnashpy <- import("nashpy")

A <- matrix(c(3, 0, 0, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
B <- matrix(c(2, 0, 0, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
game <- nashpy$Game(A, B)equilibria <- iterate(game$support_enumeration())for (eq in equilibria) {
  cat("Player 1:", eq[[1]], "\n")
  cat("Player 2:", eq[[2]], "\n\n")
}

heading: 14.2.2 Support enumeration
14.2.3 Complexity considerations
text: Support enumeration must consider \(\sum_{k=1}^{\min(m,n)} \binom{m}{k}\binom{n}{k}\) support pairs. \(3 \times 3\) game 13 pairs — manageable. \(10 \times 10\) game, count exceeds 184,000. algorithm exponential number strategies, reflecting PPAD-completeness Nash equilibrium computation (Shoham & Leyton-Brown, 2009). games roughly \(8 \times 8\), support enumeration practical; beyond , Lemke–Howson algorithm heuristics like fictitious play preferable (see 15).

heading: 14.3 Implementation in R
text: implement support enumeration pure R, handling games arbitrary size. mirrors nashpy’s core algorithm runs natively without Python.

heading: 14.3.1 General support enumeration solver
14.3.2 Testing on the three canonical 2x2 games
text: Prisoner’s Dilemma unique NE (D, D) payoffs (1, 1). Defection dominant strategy, mixed equilibrium exists.Battle Sexes three NE: two pure equilibria players coordinate ((Opera, Opera) (Football, Football)) one mixed equilibrium. mixed NE yields lower payoffs either pure NE randomization creates miscoordination risk.Matching Pennies zero-sum game pure NE. unique equilibrium requires players randomize 50-50, yielding expected payoffs (0, 0). deviation uniform mixing exploited opponent.
code:
# --- Prisoner's Dilemma ---
A_pd <- matrix(c(3, 0, 5, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("C", "D"), c("C", "D")))
B_pd <- matrix(c(3, 5, 0, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("C", "D"), c("C", "D")))

cat("--- Prisoner's Dilemma ---\n")#> --- Prisoner's Dilemma ---
cat("Player 1 payoffs:\n")#> Player 1 payoffs:
A_pd#>   C D
#> C 3 0
#> D 5 1
cat("\nPlayer 2 payoffs:\n")#> 
#> Player 2 payoffs:
B_pd#>   C D
#> C 3 5
#> D 0 1
pd_eq <- support_enumeration_general(A_pd, B_pd)
cat("\nEquilibria found:", length(pd_eq), "\n")#> 
#> Equilibria found: 1
for (eq in pd_eq) {
  cat("  p =", round(eq$p, 3), "  q =", round(eq$q, 3),
      "  payoffs = (", round(eq$payoff_1, 2), ",",
      round(eq$payoff_2, 2), ")\n")
}#>   p = 0 1   q = 0 1   payoffs = ( 1 , 1 )
# --- Battle of the Sexes ---
A_bos <- matrix(c(3, 0, 0, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                dimnames = list(c("Opera", "Football"),
                                c("Opera", "Football")))
B_bos <- matrix(c(2, 0, 0, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                dimnames = list(c("Opera", "Football"),
                                c("Opera", "Football")))

cat("--- Battle of the Sexes ---\n")#> --- Battle of the Sexes ---
bos_eq <- support_enumeration_general(A_bos, B_bos)
cat("Equilibria found:", length(bos_eq), "\n")#> Equilibria found: 3
for (eq in bos_eq) {
  is_pure <- sum(eq$p > 1e-10) == 1 && sum(eq$q > 1e-10) == 1
  label <- if (is_pure) "Pure" else "Mixed"
  cat(glue("  [{label}] p = ({paste(round(eq$p, 3), collapse=', ')})"),
      glue("q = ({paste(round(eq$q, 3), collapse=', ')})"),
      glue("payoffs = ({round(eq$payoff_1, 2)}, {round(eq$payoff_2, 2)})"),
      "\n")
}#>   [Pure] p = (1, 0) q = (1, 0) payoffs = (3, 2) 
#>   [Pure] p = (0, 1) q = (0, 1) payoffs = (2, 3) 
#>   [Mixed] p = (0.6, 0.4) q = (0.4, 0.6) payoffs = (1.2, 1.2)
# --- Matching Pennies ---
A_mp <- matrix(c(1, -1, -1, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Heads", "Tails"),
                               c("Heads", "Tails")))
B_mp <- matrix(c(-1, 1, 1, -1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Heads", "Tails"),
                               c("Heads", "Tails")))

cat("--- Matching Pennies ---\n")#> --- Matching Pennies ---
mp_eq <- support_enumeration_general(A_mp, B_mp)
cat("Equilibria found:", length(mp_eq), "\n")#> Equilibria found: 1
for (eq in mp_eq) {
  cat("  p =", round(eq$p, 3), "  q =", round(eq$q, 3),
      "  payoffs = (", round(eq$payoff_1, 2), ",",
      round(eq$payoff_2, 2), ")\n")
}#>   p = 0.5 0.5   q = 0.5 0.5   payoffs = ( 0 , 0 )

heading: 14.3.3 Cross-validation with the 2x2 solver
code:
# Verify that the general solver matches the 2x2 solver from solvers.R
cat("Cross-validation against R/solvers.R:\n\n")#> Cross-validation against R/solvers.R:
games_2x2 <- list(
  "Prisoner's Dilemma" = list(A = A_pd, B = B_pd),
  "Battle of the Sexes" = list(A = A_bos, B = B_bos),
  "Matching Pennies"    = list(A = A_mp, B = B_mp)
)

for (name in names(games_2x2)) {
  g <- games_2x2[[name]]
  eq_basic <- support_enumeration(g$A, g$B)
  eq_gen   <- support_enumeration_general(g$A, g$B)

  n_basic <- length(eq_basic$pure) + (!is.null(eq_basic$mixed))
  n_gen   <- length(eq_gen)
  cat(glue("{name}: basic solver = {n_basic} NE, general solver = {n_gen} NE"),
      "\n")
}#> Prisoner's Dilemma: basic solver = 1 NE, general solver = 1 NE 
#> Battle of the Sexes: basic solver = 3 NE, general solver = 3 NE 
#> Matching Pennies: basic solver = 1 NE, general solver = 1 NE

heading: 14.4 Worked example
text: apply general solver Hawk-Dove game (also known Chicken), extend 3x3 variant 2x2 solver R/solvers.R handle.

heading: 14.4.1 The 2x2 Hawk-Dove game
text: Two animals contest resource worth \(V = 4\). plays Hawk (aggressive) Dove (passive). play Hawk, fight receives \((V - C)/2\) cost fighting \(C = 6\). one plays Hawk Dove, Hawk takes full resource. play Dove, share equally.Hawk-Dove game two pure NE (one plays Hawk, Dove) one mixed NE player plays Hawk probability \(V/C = 2/3\). mixed equilibrium probability matches evolutionary stable strategy ??.
code:
V <- 4; C <- 6

A_hd <- matrix(c((V-C)/2, V, 0, V/2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("Hawk", "Dove"), c("Hawk", "Dove")))
B_hd <- t(A_hd)  # symmetric game
dimnames(B_hd) <- dimnames(A_hd)

cat("Hawk-Dove Game (V=4, C=6):\n")#> Hawk-Dove Game (V=4, C=6):
cat("Player 1 payoffs:\n")#> Player 1 payoffs:
A_hd#>      Hawk Dove
#> Hawk   -1    4
#> Dove    0    2
cat("\nPlayer 2 payoffs:\n")#> 
#> Player 2 payoffs:
B_hd#>      Hawk Dove
#> Hawk   -1    0
#> Dove    4    2
hd_eq <- support_enumeration_general(A_hd, B_hd)
cat("\nEquilibria found:", length(hd_eq), "\n")#> 
#> Equilibria found: 3
for (eq in hd_eq) {
  is_pure <- sum(eq$p > 1e-10) == 1 && sum(eq$q > 1e-10) == 1
  label <- if (is_pure) "Pure" else "Mixed"
  cat(glue("  [{label}] p(Hawk) = {round(eq$p[1], 4)}, ",
           "q(Hawk) = {round(eq$q[1], 4)}, ",
           "payoffs = ({round(eq$payoff_1, 3)}, {round(eq$payoff_2, 3)})"),
      "\n")
}#>   [Pure] p(Hawk) = 1, q(Hawk) = 0, payoffs = (4, 0) 
#>   [Pure] p(Hawk) = 0, q(Hawk) = 1, payoffs = (0, 4) 
#>   [Mixed] p(Hawk) = 0.6667, q(Hawk) = 0.6667, payoffs = (0.667, 0.667)

Figure 14.1: Nash equilibrium payoffs across five canonical games. point represents one equilibrium, colour distinguishing games shape distinguishing pure mixed equilibria. Battle Sexes two efficient pure NE opposite sides 45-degree line one inefficient mixed NE near origin.Zero-sum games force mixing. code:
# Collect all equilibria across games
all_games <- list(
  "Prisoner's Dilemma" = list(A = A_pd, B = B_pd),
  "Battle of Sexes"    = list(A = A_bos, B = B_bos),
  "Matching Pennies"   = list(A = A_mp, B = B_mp),

  "Rock-Paper-Scissors" = list(A = A_rps, B = B_rps)
)

eq_rows <- list()
for (game_name in names(all_games)) {
  g <- all_games[[game_name]]
  eqs <- support_enumeration_general(g$A, g$B)
  for (eq in eqs) {
    is_pure <- sum(eq$p > 1e-10) == 1 && sum(eq$q > 1e-10) == 1
    eq_rows <- c(eq_rows, list(tibble(
      game = game_name,
      type = if (is_pure) "Pure NE" else "Mixed NE",
      payoff_1 = eq$payoff_1,
      payoff_2 = eq$payoff_2
    )))
  }
}

eq_df <- bind_rows(eq_rows)

heading: 15.1 Motivation
text: solvers R/solvers.R handle 2x2 games closed-form calculation. support enumeration solver 14 extends general bimatrix games exponential number strategies. Neither approach gives us geometric insight equilibria exist structured space strategy profiles.chapter builds three algorithms scratch, offering different perspective Nash equilibrium computation:Support enumeration (recap generalization) finds equilibria exhaustive search possible support pairs. complete generic games slow large ones.Lemke–Howson algorithm follows path vertices polytope defined best-response conditions, arriving Nash equilibrium finite steps. widely used exact algorithm bimatrix games underlies Gambit’s gambit-lcp solver.Fictitious play iterative learning process: player repeatedly best-responds empirical frequency opponent’s past actions. certain conditions, empirical frequencies converge Nash equilibrium.Together three algorithms span landscape brute-force enumeration structured exact computation adaptive approximation. Building scratch demystifies “black box” equilibrium solvers prepares reader modify extend custom applications (Shoham & Leyton-Brown, 2009, Chapter 4).

heading: 15.2.1 Support enumeration (recap)
text: Support enumeration, detailed 14, exploits indifference principle 5. pair equal-sized support sets \((S_1, S_2)\), solve linear system makes player indifferent across actions opponent’s support, check non-negativity -profitable-deviation condition, collect valid solutions. algorithm exact complete non-degenerate games, running time exponential number strategies.

heading: 15.2.2 The Lemke–Howson algorithm
text:
Lemke–Howson algorithm (1964) takes geometric approach. Given \(m \times n\) bimatrix game \((, B)\) \(, B > 0\) (achieved adding constant needed), define best-response polytopes:\[
P = \{ x \\mathbb{R}^m_{\geq 0} : B^\top x \leq \mathbf{1} \}, \quad
Q = \{ y \\mathbb{R}^n_{\geq 0} : y \leq \mathbf{1} \}
\]vertex \(P \times Q\) labelled set binding constraints. pair \((x, y)\) Nash equilibrium completely labelled — union labels \(x\) \(y\) covers \(m + n\) labels \(\{1, \ldots, m+n\}\).algorithm starts artificial equilibrium \((0, 0)\) drops one label \(k\) (creating “missing label”). follows sequence edges (pivots) polytope, alternately pivoting \(P\) \(Q\), missing label picked . resulting vertex pair Nash equilibrium.key properties :Finiteness: path visits \(2^{m+n}\) vertices, algorithm terminates.Complementary pivoting: step, exactly one constraint becomes binding non-binding, analogous simplex-method pivot.Choice \(k\) matters: different starting labels \(k\) may lead different equilibria.Simplified implementationA full Lemke–Howson implementation requires careful handling degeneracy via lexicographic pivoting. implementation uses simplified tableau approach suitable small non-degenerate games. production use, solver like Gambit (see 13) preferable.

heading: 15.2.3 Fictitious play
text:
Fictitious play, introduced Brown (1951), iterative learning dynamic:player starts initial action (belief).iteration \(t\), player observes opponent’s entire history actions computes empirical frequency action.player best-responds opponent’s empirical frequency.empirical frequencies updated.Formally, let \(n_j^t\) number times column player chosen action \(j\) iteration \(t\). row player’s empirical belief column player \(\hat{q}_j^t = n_j^t / t\). row player plays:\[
a_1^{t+1} \\arg\max_i \sum_j A_{ij} \hat{q}_j^t
\]Convergence Fictitious PlayFictitious play converges Nash equilibrium following classes games:Zero-sum games (Robinson, 1951), class originally studied Neumann & Morgenstern (1944).Games solvable iterated strict dominance.\(2 \times N\) games.Games unique Nash equilibrium completely mixed.However, fictitious play converge games — Shapley (1964) constructed \(3 \times 3\) counterexample cycling behaviour (Osborne, 2004).fictitious play converges, empirical frequencies \((\hat{p}^t, \hat{q}^t)\) approach Nash equilibrium, actual actions may continue cycle. convergence time-averaged strategies, instantaneous ones.

heading: 15.3.1 Lemke–Howson sketch for 2x2 games
text: following function demonstrates complementary pivoting logic 2x2 games. illustrates algorithm’s structure rather providing production solver.
code:
lemke_howson_2x2 <- function(A, B) {
  # For 2x2 games, the Lemke-Howson path is short enough

  # that we can implement the pivoting explicitly.
  # Step 1: Check for pure strategy equilibria
  pure <- solve_2x2_pure_nash(A, B)

  # Step 2: Attempt mixed equilibrium via indifference
  mixed <- solve_2x2_mixed_nash(A, B)

  # In a full implementation, dropping label k=1 traces a path
  # through the polytope vertices. For 2x2, this reduces to
  # checking the indifference conditions directly.

  # Return the first equilibrium found
  if (length(pure) > 0) {
    eq <- pure[[1]]
    p <- rep(0, 2)
    q <- rep(0, 2)
    p[eq[1]] <- 1
    q[eq[2]] <- 1
    return(list(p = p, q = q, type = "pure",
                payoff_1 = A[eq[1], eq[2]],
                payoff_2 = B[eq[1], eq[2]]))
  }
  if (!is.null(mixed)) {
    p <- c(mixed$p, 1 - mixed$p)
    q <- c(mixed$q, 1 - mixed$q)
    payoff_1 <- as.numeric(p %*% A %*% q)
    payoff_2 <- as.numeric(p %*% B %*% q)
    return(list(p = p, q = q, type = "mixed",
                payoff_1 = payoff_1, payoff_2 = payoff_2))
  }
  NULL
}

# Test on Matching Pennies
A_mp <- matrix(c(1, -1, -1, 1), 2, 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("H", "T"), c("H", "T")))
B_mp <- matrix(c(-1, 1, 1, -1), 2, 2, byrow = TRUE,
               dimnames = list(c("H", "T"), c("H", "T")))

lh_result <- lemke_howson_2x2(A_mp, B_mp)
cat("Lemke-Howson result for Matching Pennies:\n")#> Lemke-Howson result for Matching Pennies:
cat("  p =", lh_result$p, "(type:", lh_result$type, ")\n")#>   p = 0.5 0.5 (type: mixed )
cat("  q =", lh_result$q, "\n")#>   q = 0.5 0.5
cat(sprintf("  Payoffs: (%.2f, %.2f)\n", lh_result$payoff_1, lh_result$payoff_2))#>   Payoffs: (0.00, 0.00)

heading: 15.3.2 Fictitious play implementation
code:
fictitious_play <- function(A, B, n_iter = 1000, seed = 42) {
  set.seed(seed)
  m <- nrow(A)
  n <- ncol(A)

  # Counts of each action played
  row_counts <- rep(0, m)
  col_counts <- rep(0, n)

  # Initial actions (random)
  row_action <- sample(m, 1)
  col_action <- sample(n, 1)
  row_counts[row_action] <- 1
  col_counts[col_action] <- 1

  # Storage for trajectory (subsample for efficiency)
  record_iters <- c(seq_len(50), seq(60, min(n_iter, 500), by = 10),
                     seq(600, n_iter, by = 100))
  record_iters <- sort(unique(record_iters[record_iters <= n_iter]))

  history <- tibble(
    iter = integer(),
    row_freq_1 = double(),
    col_freq_1 = double()
  )

  for (t in 2:n_iter) {
    # Row player best-responds to column player's empirical frequency
    col_freq <- col_counts / sum(col_counts)
    row_expected <- as.numeric(A %*% col_freq)
    best_rows <- which(abs(row_expected - max(row_expected)) < 1e-12)
    row_action <- if (length(best_rows) > 1) sample(best_rows, 1) else best_rows

    # Column player best-responds to row player's empirical frequency
    row_freq <- row_counts / sum(row_counts)
    col_expected <- as.numeric(t(B) %*% row_freq)
    best_cols <- which(abs(col_expected - max(col_expected)) < 1e-12)
    col_action <- if (length(best_cols) > 1) sample(best_cols, 1) else best_cols

    # Update counts
    row_counts[row_action] <- row_counts[row_action] + 1
    col_counts[col_action] <- col_counts[col_action] + 1

    # Record at selected iterations
    if (t %in% record_iters) {
      row_freq_now <- row_counts / sum(row_counts)
      col_freq_now <- col_counts / sum(col_counts)
      history <- bind_rows(history, tibble(
        iter = t,
        row_freq_1 = row_freq_now[1],
        col_freq_1 = col_freq_now[1]
      ))
    }
  }

  # Final empirical frequencies
  final_row_freq <- row_counts / sum(row_counts)
  final_col_freq <- col_counts / sum(col_counts)

  list(
    p = final_row_freq,
    q = final_col_freq,
    payoff_1 = as.numeric(final_row_freq %*% A %*% final_col_freq),
    payoff_2 = as.numeric(final_row_freq %*% B %*% final_col_freq),
    history = history,
    row_counts = row_counts,
    col_counts = col_counts
  )
}

heading: 15.4.1 Setting up the game

# Battle of the Sexes
A_bos <- matrix(c(3, 0, 0, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                dimnames = list(c("Opera", "Football"), c("Opera", "Football")))
B_bos <- matrix(c(2, 0, 0, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                dimnames = list(c("Opera", "Football"), c("Opera", "Football")))

# True mixed NE: p = 3/5 (P1 plays Opera), q = 2/5 (P2 plays Opera)
true_p <- 3 / 5
true_q <- 2 / 5

cat("Battle of the Sexes payoffs:\n")#> Battle of the Sexes payoffs:
cat("Player 1:\n")#> Player 1:
A_bos#>          Opera Football
#> Opera        3        0
#> Football     0        2
cat("\nPlayer 2:\n")#> 
#> Player 2:
B_bos#>          Opera Football
#> Opera        2        0
#> Football     0        3
cat(sprintf("\nTrue mixed NE: p(Opera) = %.2f, q(Opera) = %.2f\n", true_p, true_q))#>

heading: 15.4.2 Running fictitious play
text: empirical frequencies approximate mixed NE \((p, q) = (0.6, 0.4)\). convergence slow — fictitious play converges rate \(O(1/\sqrt{t})\) — robust requires matrix inversion linear programming.
code:
# Run fictitious play with many iterations
fp_bos <- fictitious_play(A_bos, B_bos, n_iter = 5000)

cat("Battle of the Sexes --- Fictitious Play Results:\n")#> Battle of the Sexes --- Fictitious Play Results:
cat("  Empirical p(Opera) =", round(fp_bos$p[1], 4),
    "  (true:", true_p, ")\n")#>   Empirical p(Opera) = 1   (true: 0.6 )
cat("  Empirical q(Opera) =", round(fp_bos$q[1], 4),
    "  (true:", true_q, ")\n\n")#>   Empirical q(Opera) = 1   (true: 0.4 )
# Expected payoffs at the mixed NE
mixed_payoff_1 <- true_p * true_q * 3 + (1 - true_p) * (1 - true_q) * 2
mixed_payoff_2 <- true_p * true_q * 2 + (1 - true_p) * (1 - true_q) * 3
cat("Mixed NE expected payoffs:", round(mixed_payoff_1, 3), ",",
    round(mixed_payoff_2, 3), "\n")#> Mixed NE expected payoffs: 1.2 , 1.2
cat("Fictitious play payoffs:  ", round(fp_bos$payoff_1, 3), ",",
    round(fp_bos$payoff_2, 3), "\n")#> Fictitious play payoffs:   3 , 2

heading: 15.4.3 Publication figure: convergence trajectory
text:
Figure 15.1: Fictitious play convergence Battle Sexes. trajectory empirical action frequencies (Player 1 plays Opera x-axis, Player 2 plays Opera y-axis) spirals toward mixed Nash equilibrium (0.6, 0.4). Early iterations show large oscillations players alternate best responses; later iterations settle near equilibrium. two pure Nash equilibria (1, 1) (0, 0) shown reference points.
spiral pattern figure characteristic fictitious play games mixed equilibria. player’s best response ’s current empirical frequency overshoots equilibrium, creating oscillations gradually dampen. early phase (orange) shows wild swings; middle phase (blue) tightens spiral; late phase (green) clusters near mixed NE \((0.6, 0.4)\).convergence pattern explains fictitious play, despite simplicity, useful computational tool model bounded rationality. Players need know game’s payoff structure full — need observe past actions best-respond. path equilibrium emerges simple learning rule, without explicit computation indifference conditions 5.
code:
# Build trajectory data for the plot
trajectory <- fp_bos$history |>
  mutate(
    phase = case_when(
      iter <= 100  ~ "Early (t <= 100)",
      iter <= 1000 ~ "Middle (100 < t <= 1000)",
      TRUE         ~ "Late (t > 1000)"
    ),
    phase = factor(phase, levels = c("Early (t <= 100)",
                                      "Middle (100 < t <= 1000)",
                                      "Late (t > 1000)"))
  )

# Reference points: pure NE and mixed NE
ref_points <- tibble(
  x = c(1, 0, true_p),
  y = c(1, 0, true_q),
  label = c("Pure NE: (Opera, Opera)", "Pure NE: (Football, Football)",
            "Mixed NE (0.6, 0.4)")
)

p_fp <- ggplot() +
  # Trajectory coloured by phase
  geom_path(data = trajectory,
            aes(x = row_freq_1, y = col_freq_1, colour = phase),
            linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  # Starting point
  geom_point(data = slice_head(trajectory, n = 1),
             aes(x = row_freq_1, y = col_freq_1),
             shape = 4, size = 3, stroke = 1.5, colour = "black") +
  # Final point
  geom_point(data = slice_tail(trajectory, n = 1),
             aes(x = row_freq_1, y = col_freq_1),
             shape = 8, size = 3, stroke = 1.5, colour = "black") +
  # Reference equilibria
  geom_point(data = ref_points, aes(x = x, y = y),
             shape = 18, size = 4, colour = okabe_ito[6]) +
  geom_text(data = ref_points, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
            vjust = -1, size = 2.7, colour = okabe_ito[6]) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(okabe_ito[1], okabe_ito[2], okabe_ito[3]),
    name = "Iteration phase"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Player 1: empirical freq. of Opera",
    limits = c(-0.05, 1.1),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Player 2: empirical freq. of Opera",
    limits = c(-0.05, 1.15),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)
  ) +
  labs(title = "Fictitious Play Convergence: Battle of the Sexes") +
  theme_publication()

p_fp
save_pub_fig(p_fp, "fictitious-play-convergence", width = 7, height = 6)

heading: 15.4.4 Comparing all three approaches
text: now apply three algorithms Battle Sexes compare outputs.comparison reveals key distinction. Lemke–Howson exact algebraic method finds one equilibrium (, pure NE). Fictitious play iterative approximation converges toward mixed equilibrium. Support enumeration method finds equilibria. choice among depends analyst’s goals: completeness favours support enumeration, speed favours Lemke–Howson, simplicity favours fictitious play.
code:
# 1. Lemke-Howson sketch
lh_bos <- lemke_howson_2x2(A_bos, B_bos)
cat("Lemke-Howson:\n")#> Lemke-Howson:
cat("  p =", round(lh_bos$p, 4), " q =", round(lh_bos$q, 4),
    " type:", lh_bos$type, "\n")#>   p = 1 0  q = 1 0  type: pure
cat(sprintf("  Payoffs: (%.2f, %.2f)\n\n", lh_bos$payoff_1, lh_bos$payoff_2))#>   Payoffs: (3.00, 2.00)
# 2. Fictitious play (already computed above)
cat("Fictitious play (5000 iterations):\n")#> Fictitious play (5000 iterations):
cat("  p =", round(fp_bos$p, 4), " q =", round(fp_bos$q, 4), "\n")#>   p = 1 0  q = 1 0
cat(sprintf("  Payoffs: (%.2f, %.2f)\n\n", fp_bos$payoff_1, fp_bos$payoff_2))#>   Payoffs: (3.00, 2.00)
# 3. Exact support enumeration (from \@ref(sec-nashpy-reticulate))
cat("Exact solutions (for reference):\n")#> Exact solutions (for reference):
pure_ne <- solve_2x2_pure_nash(A_bos, B_bos)
mixed_ne <- solve_2x2_mixed_nash(A_bos, B_bos)
for (eq in pure_ne) {
  cat(sprintf("  Pure NE: (%s, %s) -> payoffs (%d, %d)\n",
              rownames(A_bos)[eq[1]], colnames(A_bos)[eq[2]],
              A_bos[eq[1], eq[2]], B_bos[eq[1], eq[2]]))
}#>   Pure NE: (Opera, Opera) -> payoffs (3, 2)
#>   Pure NE: (Football, Football) -> payoffs (2, 3)
if (!is.null(mixed_ne)) {
  p_exact <- c(mixed_ne$p, 1 - mixed_ne$p)
  q_exact <- c(mixed_ne$q, 1 - mixed_ne$q)
  payoff_1_exact <- as.numeric(p_exact %*% A_bos %*% q_exact)
  payoff_2_exact <- as.numeric(p_exact %*% B_bos %*% q_exact)
  cat(sprintf("  Mixed NE: p = (%.3f, %.3f), q = (%.3f, %.3f) -> payoffs (%.2f, %.2f)\n",
              p_exact[1], p_exact[2], q_exact[1], q_exact[2],
              payoff_1_exact, payoff_2_exact))
}#>   Mixed NE: p = (0.600, 0.400), q = (0.400, 0.600) -> payoffs (1.20, 1.20)

heading: 15.4.5 Convergence speed across games
text: games show expected \(O(1/\sqrt{t})\) convergence rate (approximately linear log-log scale slope around \(-0.5\)). Matching Pennies, zero-sum, converges smoothly. Battle Sexes shows irregular convergence due coordination structure presence multiple equilibria.
code:
# Run fictitious play on two games and track convergence
games_for_fp <- list(
  "Matching Pennies" = list(A = A_mp, B = B_mp, true_p = 0.5, true_q = 0.5),
  "Battle of Sexes"  = list(A = A_bos, B = B_bos, true_p = 0.6, true_q = 0.4)
)

convergence_data <- list()
for (game_name in names(games_for_fp)) {
  g <- games_for_fp[[game_name]]
  fp <- fictitious_play(g$A, g$B, n_iter = 5000)

  conv <- fp$history |>
    mutate(
      error_p = abs(row_freq_1 - g$true_p),
      error_q = abs(col_freq_1 - g$true_q),
      total_error = error_p + error_q,
      game = game_name
    )
  convergence_data <- c(convergence_data, list(conv))
}

conv_df <- bind_rows(convergence_data)

conv_df |>
  filter(iter >= 10) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = iter, y = total_error, colour = game)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = okabe_ito[c(1, 2)]) +
  scale_x_log10(name = "Iteration", labels = label_comma()) +
  scale_y_log10(name = "Total frequency error |p - p*| + |q - q*|") +
  labs(
    title = "Fictitious Play Convergence Speed",
    colour = "Game"
  ) +
  theme_publication()

heading: 16.3.1 Mixed-strategy payoff estimation
text: begin \(2 \times 2\) game players use mixed strategies. Consider Matching Pennies, unique Nash equilibrium players mixing uniformly Heads Tails.
code:
# Matching Pennies payoff matrix (row player)
# Row = Heads/Tails, Col = Heads/Tails
payoff_row <- matrix(c(1, -1, -1, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                     dimnames = list(c("H", "T"), c("H", "T")))

# Nash equilibrium: both players mix 50-50
p_row <- c(H = 0.5, T = 0.5)
p_col <- c(H = 0.5, T = 0.5)

# Analytical expected payoff under NE
analytical_payoff <- sum(outer(p_row, p_col) * payoff_row)
cat(glue("Analytical expected payoff (row player): {analytical_payoff}"), "\n")#> Analytical expected payoff (row player): 0
# Monte Carlo estimation of the expected payoff
simulate_payoffs <- function(payoff_matrix, p_row, p_col, n_sims) {
  row_actions <- sample(rownames(payoff_matrix), n_sims, replace = TRUE,
                        prob = p_row)
  col_actions <- sample(colnames(payoff_matrix), n_sims, replace = TRUE,
                        prob = p_col)
  payoffs <- mapply(function(r, c) payoff_matrix[r, c],
                    row_actions, col_actions)
  payoffs
}

set.seed(42)
payoffs_10k <- simulate_payoffs(payoff_row, p_row, p_col, n_sims = 10000)

cat(glue("MC estimate (10,000 samples): {round(mean(payoffs_10k), 4)}"), "\n")#> MC estimate (10,000 samples): -0.001
cat(glue("MC std error: {round(sd(payoffs_10k) / sqrt(length(payoffs_10k)), 4)}"), "\n")#> MC std error: 0.01

heading: 16.3.2 Convergence plot
text: Figure 16.1: Convergence Monte Carlo payoff estimate analytical solution (dashed line zero) Matching Pennies Nash equilibrium play. shaded ribbon shows 95% confidence interval, narrows rate \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\).
code:
set.seed(42)
n_total <- 10000
payoffs_all <- simulate_payoffs(payoff_row, p_row, p_col, n_total)

# Compute running mean and CI at selected sample sizes
sample_sizes <- c(seq(10, 100, by = 10), seq(150, 1000, by = 50),
                  seq(1200, 10000, by = 200))

convergence_df <- map_dfr(sample_sizes, function(n) {
  x <- payoffs_all[1:n]
  mu <- mean(x)
  se <- sd(x) / sqrt(n)
  tibble(n = n, estimate = mu, ci_lower = mu - 1.96 * se,
         ci_upper = mu + 1.96 * se)
})

p_convergence <- ggplot(convergence_df, aes(x = n, y = estimate)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper),
              fill = okabe_ito[2], alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(colour = okabe_ito[1], linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = analytical_payoff, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = okabe_ito[8], linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  theme_publication() +
  labs(title = "Monte Carlo Convergence: Matching Pennies",
       x = "Number of samples (log scale)",
       y = "Estimated expected payoff",
       caption = "Dashed line = analytical expected payoff (0)")

p_convergence
save_pub_fig(p_convergence, "mc-convergence", width = 7, height = 5)

Now consider asymmetric game payoff distribution interesting. use modified Battle Sexes mixed-strategy equilibrium.

# Battle of the Sexes payoff matrix (row player)
payoff_bos <- matrix(c(3, 0, 0, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                     dimnames = list(c("Opera", "Football"),
                                     c("Opera", "Football")))

# Mixed NE: row plays Opera with prob 2/5, Football with prob 3/5
# Col plays Opera with prob 3/5, Football with prob 2/5

p_col_bos <- c(Opera = 3/5, Football = 2/5)

                 fill = okabe_ito[3], colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +

       x = "Row-player payoff",
       y = "Density",

p_distribution

heading: 16.4 Worked example
text:
now apply Monte Carlo estimation simplified poker-like game. Two players receive one card deck integers 1 10 (dealt without replacement). Player 1 observes card decides bet check. Player 1 checks, payoffs zero . Player 1 bets, Player 2 (also seen card) decides call fold. Player 2 folds, Player 1 wins pot 1. Player 2 calls, higher card wins pot 2 (loser pays 1).Player 1’s strategy: bet card \(\geq k_1\) (threshold strategy cutoff \(k_1\)).
Player 2’s strategy: call card \(\geq k_2\) (threshold strategy cutoff \(k_2\)).
code:
play_poker_round <- function(k1, k2, deck_size = 10) {
  cards <- sample(deck_size, 2, replace = FALSE)
  card1 <- cards[1]
  card2 <- cards[2]

  # Player 1 decision

  if (card1 < k1) {
    return(0)  # check, no payoff
  }

  # Player 1 bets; Player 2 decision
  if (card2 < k2) {
    return(1)  # Player 2 folds, Player 1 wins pot of 1
  }

  # Showdown
  if (card1 > card2) return(2)   # Player 1 wins pot of 2
  if (card1 < card2) return(-1)  # Player 1 loses 1
  return(0)                       # tie
}

# Evaluate Player 1's expected payoff for different threshold combinations
set.seed(42)
n_sims <- 20000

threshold_grid <- expand_grid(k1 = 1:10, k2 = 1:10)

results <- threshold_grid |>
  mutate(
    payoffs = map2(k1, k2, function(k1, k2) {
      replicate(n_sims, play_poker_round(k1, k2))
    }),
    mean_payoff = map_dbl(payoffs, mean),
    se = map_dbl(payoffs, ~ sd(.x) / sqrt(length(.x)))
  )

# Find the best threshold for Player 1 (assuming Player 2 plays optimally)
# For each k1, find k2 that minimizes Player 1's payoff (Player 2's best response)
best_responses <- results |>
  group_by(k1) |>
  summarise(
    min_payoff = min(mean_payoff),
    best_k2 = k2[which.min(mean_payoff)],
    .groups = "drop"
  )

optimal_k1 <- best_responses |>
  filter(min_payoff == max(min_payoff))

cat("Player 1's maximin strategy:\n")#> Player 1's maximin strategy:
cat(glue("  Bet threshold: k1 >= {optimal_k1$k1}"), "\n")#>   Bet threshold: k1 >= 4
cat(glue("  Player 2 best response: call if k2 >= {optimal_k1$best_k2}"), "\n")#>   Player 2 best response: call if k2 >= 7
cat(glue("  Expected payoff: {round(optimal_k1$min_payoff, 4)}"), "\n")#>   Expected payoff: 0.362

heading: 16.4.1 Bootstrap confidence interval
text: use bootstrap construct confidence interval Player 1’s expected payoff estimated optimal strategies.
code:
set.seed(42)
k1_star <- optimal_k1$k1[1]
k2_star <- optimal_k1$best_k2[1]

# Generate payoff samples under optimal play
payoff_samples <- replicate(20000, play_poker_round(k1_star, k2_star))

# Bootstrap
B <- 5000
boot_means <- replicate(B, {
  resample <- sample(payoff_samples, replace = TRUE)
  mean(resample)
})

ci_boot <- quantile(boot_means, c(0.025, 0.975))

cat("Bootstrap 95% CI for Player 1's expected payoff:\n")#> Bootstrap 95% CI for Player 1's expected payoff:
cat(glue("  [{round(ci_boot[1], 4)}, {round(ci_boot[2], 4)}]"), "\n")#>   [0.3626, 0.387]
cat(glue("  Point estimate: {round(mean(payoff_samples), 4)}"), "\n")#>   Point estimate: 0.3747

heading: 16.4.2 Verifying convergence
text: verify Monte Carlo estimate stabilizes sample size grows:table confirms \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\) convergence: quadrupling sample size roughly halves confidence interval width, consistent (16.3).
code:
check_sizes <- c(100, 500, 1000, 5000, 10000, 20000)

convergence_check <- map_dfr(check_sizes, function(n) {
  x <- payoff_samples[1:n]
  tibble(
    n = n,
    estimate = mean(x),
    se = sd(x) / sqrt(n),
    ci_width = 2 * 1.96 * sd(x) / sqrt(n)
  )
})

convergence_check |>
  mutate(across(c(estimate, se, ci_width), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(n = "Samples", estimate = "Estimate", se = "Std Error",
             ci_width = "95% CI Width") |>
  tab_header(title = "Convergence of Monte Carlo Estimate",
             subtitle = "Simplified poker game under estimated optimal play")

heading: 17 Agent-Based Models for Strategic Interaction
text: Building agent-based models heterogeneous agents play games, reproduce proportional fitness, evolve strategies generations — illustrated Hawk-Dove game.

heading: Learning objectives
text: Contrast agent-based modeling paradigm analytical equilibrium analysis.Implement population agents heterogeneous strategies interact pairwise games.Model evolutionary pressure via fitness-proportional reproduction mutation.Simulate Hawk-Dove game interpret emergent strategy frequencies terms evolutionarily stable strategy.

heading: 17.1 Motivation
text: Analytical game theory derives equilibria assuming fully rational players common knowledge. powerful sometimes unrealistic. biology, economics, social science, often observe populations agents limited information, heterogeneous strategies, adaptive behavior. strategy frequencies evolve agents interact locally, reproduce based success, occasionally mutate?Agent-based models (ABMs) answer question computationally. Instead solving equilibrium, simulate . agent carries strategy, plays games others, earns payoffs, population evolves generations. ABMs can reveal dynamics analytical models miss: path dependence, transient oscillations, role population size, effect mutation rates. Maynard Smith’s Hawk-Dove game — cornerstone evolutionary game theory — provides ideal testbed known analytical equilibrium can compare (Osborne, 2004).

heading: 17.2.1 The Hawk-Dove game
17.2.2 Agent-based evolutionary dynamics
17.2.3 Connection to replicator dynamics
17.3.1 Core simulation functions
17.3.2 Running the ABM
code:
run_hawk_dove_abm <- function(n_agents = 200, n_generations = 300,
                               initial_hawk_freq = 0.5,
                               n_interactions = 10,
                               mutation_rate = 0.01,
                               v = 2, c = 5, seed = 42) {
  set.seed(seed)

  # Initialize population
  n_hawks <- round(n_agents * initial_hawk_freq)
  population <- c(rep("Hawk", n_hawks), rep("Dove", n_agents - n_hawks))

  # Storage for tracking
  history <- tibble(
    generation = integer(),
    hawk_freq = double(),
    mean_fitness = double()
  )

  for (gen in seq_len(n_generations)) {
    hawk_freq <- mean(population == "Hawk")
    fitness <- compute_fitness(population, n_interactions, v, c)

    history <- bind_rows(history, tibble(
      generation = gen,
      hawk_freq = hawk_freq,
      mean_fitness = mean(fitness)
    ))

    # Selection and reproduction
    population <- select_parents(population, fitness, n_agents)

    # Mutation
    population <- mutate_population(population, mutation_rate)
  }

  history
}

heading: 17.3.3 Strategy frequency evolution
text: Figure 17.1: Strategy frequency evolution Hawk-Dove agent-based model across three independent runs. dashed horizontal line marks analytical ESS frequency \(p^* = v/c = 0.4\). Despite starting different initial conditions, runs converge fluctuate around predicted equilibrium.
code:
v <- 2
c_val <- 5
ess_freq <- v / c_val

# Run three independent simulations with different initial conditions
runs <- list(
  list(init = 0.1, seed = 42, label = "Run 1 (10% Hawk)"),
  list(init = 0.5, seed = 123, label = "Run 2 (50% Hawk)"),
  list(init = 0.9, seed = 456, label = "Run 3 (90% Hawk)")
)

history_all <- map_dfr(runs, function(r) {
  run_hawk_dove_abm(
    n_agents = 200, n_generations = 300,
    initial_hawk_freq = r$init, n_interactions = 10,
    mutation_rate = 0.01, v = v, c = c_val, seed = r$seed
  ) |>
    mutate(run = r$label)
})

p_evolution <- ggplot(history_all, aes(x = generation, y = hawk_freq,
                                        colour = run)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = ess_freq, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = okabe_ito[8], linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = okabe_ito[c(1, 2, 3)], name = "Simulation") +
  theme_publication() +
  labs(title = "Hawk-Dove ABM: Strategy Frequency Over Generations",
       x = "Generation",
       y = "Fraction playing Hawk",
       caption = glue("Dashed line = ESS frequency (v/c = {ess_freq})"))

p_evolution
save_pub_fig(p_evolution, "abm-strategy-evolution", width = 7, height = 5)

heading: 17.3.4 Population fitness over time
text: Figure 17.2: Average population fitness generations Hawk-Dove ABM. Fitness highest population near ESS. Early generations show rapid fitness changes population adjusts initial state; later generations fluctuate around equilibrium fitness level.
code:
# Compute the analytical equilibrium fitness
# At ESS: pi_bar = p*[p*(v-c)/2 + (1-p*)v] + (1-p*)[p*0 + (1-p*)v/2]
p_star <- ess_freq
pi_bar_star <- p_star * (p_star * (v - c_val)/2 + (1 - p_star) * v) +
               (1 - p_star) * (p_star * 0 + (1 - p_star) * v/2)

p_fitness <- ggplot(history_all, aes(x = generation, y = mean_fitness,
                                      colour = run)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = pi_bar_star, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = okabe_ito[8], linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = okabe_ito[c(1, 2, 3)], name = "Simulation") +
  theme_publication() +
  labs(title = "Hawk-Dove ABM: Average Population Fitness",
       x = "Generation",
       y = "Mean payoff per interaction",
       caption = glue("Dashed line = fitness at ESS ({round(pi_bar_star, 2)})"))

p_fitness
save_pub_fig(p_fitness, "abm-fitness", width = 7, height = 5)

heading: 17.4 Worked example
text: Let us trace first generations small population understand mechanics.population moves toward ESS. Hawks overrepresented (70% vs. ESS 40%), frequently fight incur costly loss \((v-c)/2 = -1.5\). Doves, meanwhile, avoid fighting costs entirely. Selection therefore favors Doves, pulling population toward equilibrium frequency.
code:
set.seed(42)
n_agents <- 20
population <- c(rep("Hawk", 14), rep("Dove", 6))  # 70% Hawk

cat("Initial population (20 agents, 70% Hawk):\n")#> Initial population (20 agents, 70% Hawk):
cat(glue("  Hawks: {sum(population == 'Hawk')}, ",
         "Doves: {sum(population == 'Dove')}"), "\n\n")#>   Hawks: 14, Doves: 6
# Generation 1: compute fitness
fitness <- compute_fitness(population, n_interactions = 5, v = v, c = c_val)

fitness_by_type <- tibble(
  strategy = population,
  fitness = fitness
) |>
  group_by(strategy) |>
  summarise(mean_fitness = round(mean(fitness), 3),
            min_fitness = round(min(fitness), 3),
            max_fitness = round(max(fitness), 3),
            .groups = "drop")

cat("Generation 1 fitness summary:\n")#> Generation 1 fitness summary:

heading: 17.4.1 Sensitivity to parameters
text: Higher mutation rates increase variance around ESS — agents frequently pushed away equilibrium — mean frequency remains close \(p^* = 0.4\) across mutation rates. robustness signature ESS: selection pressure always pushes back toward equilibrium, regardless perturbations (Neumann & Morgenstern, 1944).
code:
# Effect of mutation rate on variance of equilibrium frequency
mutation_rates <- c(0.001, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1)

sensitivity_df <- map_dfr(mutation_rates, function(mu) {
  h <- run_hawk_dove_abm(n_agents = 200, n_generations = 300,
                          initial_hawk_freq = 0.5, n_interactions = 10,
                          mutation_rate = mu, v = v, c = c_val, seed = 42)
  # Use last 100 generations as "steady state"
  steady <- tail(h, 100)
  tibble(
    mutation_rate = mu,
    mean_hawk_freq = round(mean(steady$hawk_freq), 3),
    sd_hawk_freq = round(sd(steady$hawk_freq), 3)
  )
})

sensitivity_df |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(mutation_rate = "Mutation rate",
             mean_hawk_freq = "Mean Hawk freq",
             sd_hawk_freq = "SD of Hawk freq") |>
  tab_header(title = "Effect of Mutation Rate on Equilibrium Behavior",
             subtitle = "Steady-state statistics from last 100 of 300 generations")

heading: 18 Replicating Axelrod’s Tournament
text: computational replication Axelrod’s 1980 iterated Prisoner’s Dilemma tournament R, exploring Tit Tat won teaches evolution cooperation.

heading: Learning objectives

heading: 18.1 Motivation
text: 1980, political scientist Robert Axelrod invited game theorists submit strategies iterated Prisoner’s Dilemma computer tournament. strategy play every strategy () round-robin, accumulating points 200 rounds per match. submissions ranged simple elaborate — used sophisticated pattern recognition, others just lines code.winner simplest strategy entered: Tit Tat, submitted Anatol Rapoport. cooperates first move copies whatever opponent previous move. Axelrod (1981) ran second tournament publishing first results, inviting even entries knowledge first outcome. Tit Tat won .result — strategy requiring memory beyond last round, exploitation, complex reasoning outperform sophisticated alternatives — became one influential findings study cooperation. chapter, replicate tournament R.

heading: 18.2.1 The iterated Prisoner’s Dilemma
text: stage game payoffs follow standard convention:Prisoner’s Dilemma payoff structure \(T > R > P > S\) \(2R > T + S\).canonical values \(T = 5\), \(R = 3\), \(P = 1\), \(S = 0\): temptation defect high, mutual cooperation (\(R = 3\)) beats average exploitation exploited (\((T + S)/2 = 2.5\)).game repeated, players can condition current action history play. opens door conditional cooperation: player might cooperate long opponent cooperates, punish defection.

heading: 18.2.2 Axelrod’s four properties of successful strategies
text: Analyzing tournament results, Axelrod identified four properties shared top-performing strategies:Nice — never first defect.Retaliatory — punish defection promptly.Forgiving — return cooperation opponent .Clear — behavior predictable, enabling mutual cooperation.Tit Tat exemplifies four: never defects first (nice), defects immediately opponent (retaliatory), cooperates immediately opponent returns cooperation (forgiving), logic transparent (clear).

heading: 18.3.1 Strategy definitions
text: define library classic strategies. strategy function takes two arguments — player’s history opponent’s history — returns "C" "D".

heading: 18.3.2 Round-robin tournament
code:
run_tournament <- function(strategies, rounds = 200) {
  names_s <- names(strategies)
  n <- length(strategies)
  results <- matrix(0, nrow = n, ncol = n,
                    dimnames = list(names_s, names_s))
  for (i in seq_len(n)) {
    for (j in seq(i, n)) {
      scores <- run_match(strategies[[i]], strategies[[j]], rounds)
      results[i, j] <- results[i, j] + scores[1]
      results[j, i] <- results[j, i] + scores[2]
    }
  }
  results
}

set.seed(42)
results <- run_tournament(strategies, rounds = 200)

totals <- rowSums(results)
ranking <- sort(totals, decreasing = TRUE)

cat("Tournament Results (total scores):\n\n")#> Tournament Results (total scores):
for (i in seq_along(ranking)) {
  cat(sprintf("  %d. %-20s %5d\n", i, names(ranking)[i], ranking[i]))
}#>   1. Tit for Two Tats      4761
#>   2. Tit for Tat           4725
#>   3. Grudger               4585
#>   4. Pavlov                4538
#>   5. Always Cooperate      4518
#>   6. Random                3900
#>   7. Always Defect         3416
#>   8. Suspicious TFT        3372

heading: 19 Spatial Prisoner’s Dilemma
text: computational exploration Nowak May’s spatial Prisoner’s Dilemma, showing local interaction lattice allows cooperation survive cluster formation — even without memory, reciprocity, reputation.

heading: Learning objectives
text: Explain spatial structure can sustain cooperation Prisoner’s Dilemma without reciprocity punishment.Implement lattice-based spatial PD Moore neighborhood interaction imitation dynamics R.Visualize emergence persistence cooperator clusters grid.Analyze fraction cooperators evolves time different payoff parameters.

heading: 19.1 Motivation
text: 18, cooperation emerged players remember past interactions condition behavior history. many biological social systems lack luxury. Bacteria, plants, even commuters highway interact nearby neighbors without tracking individual histories. Can cooperation survive memory, reputation, punishment — spatial structure?1992, Martin Nowak Robert May (1992) showed answer yes. placed players two-dimensional grid cell plays one-shot Prisoner’s Dilemma immediate neighbors. round, every cell adopts strategy whichever neighbor (including ) scored highest. result striking: cooperators formed compact clusters resist invasion defectors, producing intricate, dynamic spatial patterns.chapter replicates model R. build lattice simulation, watch cooperation clusters emerge, measure population-level cooperation rates evolve time.

heading: 19.2.1 From well-mixed to spatial populations
text: Standard evolutionary game theory assumes well-mixed population: every individual equally likely interact every . assumption, defection dominates one-shot Prisoner’s Dilemma defector always earns cooperator given opponent.Spatial structure breaks assumption. interaction local — player meets geographic neighbors — cooperators can form clusters. Within cluster, cooperators interact mainly cooperators, earning mutual cooperation payoff \(R\) many times. defector boundary cluster exploits cooperator neighbors fewer cooperator neighbors cooperators cluster interior .

heading: 19.2.2 The Nowak–May model
text: model works follows:Grid: \(N \times N\) lattice periodic boundary conditions (torus, edges wrap around).Strategies: cell either cooperator (C) defector (D).Interaction: cell plays one-shot PD eight Moore neighbors (nine games total). cell’s score sum payoffs nine games.Update: Synchronously, every cell adopts strategy highest-scoring cell Moore neighborhood (including ). Ties broken favor current occupant.canonical PD payoffs (\(T > R > P > S\)), dynamics depend critically temptation--defect parameter \(b = T/R\). moderate values \(b\), cooperators defectors coexist dynamic spatial patterns. high \(b\), defection takes ; low \(b\), cooperation dominates.

heading: 19.2.3 Why clusters protect cooperators
text: Consider \(3 \times 3\) block cooperators surrounded defectors. cooperator center plays nine cooperators (including ), scoring \(9R\). defector adjacent block three cooperator neighbors, scoring \(3T + 6P\). \(9R > 3T + 6P\) — , \(b < 3(R - P/R) + P\) approximately — interior cooperator outscores boundary defector, cluster holds. geometric logic behind spatial cooperation.

heading: 19.3.1 Grid initialization and neighbor scoring
19.3.2 Update rule: imitate the best neighbor
19.3.3 Running the simulation
code:
run_spatial_pd <- function(n, generations, prob_coop = 0.5, seed = 42) {
  set.seed(seed)
  grid <- initialize_grid(n, prob_coop)

  history <- vector("list", generations + 1)
  history[[1]] <- grid
  coop_frac <- numeric(generations + 1)
  coop_frac[1] <- mean(grid == "C")

  for (g in seq_len(generations)) {
    scores <- compute_scores(grid)
    grid <- update_grid(grid, scores)
    history[[g + 1]] <- grid
    coop_frac[g + 1] <- mean(grid == "C")
  }

  list(history = history, coop_frac = coop_frac)
}

heading: 20 Replicator Dynamics
text: introduction replicator equation — central dynamical system evolutionary game theory — ODE solutions R Rock-Paper-Scissors Hawk-Dove games, including simplex visualization.

heading: Learning objectives
text: State replicator equation explain biological game-theoretic intuition behind term.Use deSolve numerically integrate replicator equation arbitrary payoff matrices.Visualize replicator dynamics two-dimensional simplex three-strategy games.Analyze convergence equilibrium Hawk-Dove cycling behavior Rock-Paper-Scissors.

heading: 20.1 Motivation
text: 18 19, studied discrete agents making discrete choices. many evolutionary economic processes better described continuous time: strategy frequencies shift gradually successful types reproduce faster. model ?replicator equation, introduced Taylor Jonker (1978) named Schuster Sigmund (1983), provides answer. fundamental dynamical system evolutionary game theory, describing frequency strategy large population changes time function fitness relative population average.replicator equation connects game theory biology (arises natural selection), economics (describes learning dynamics large populations), dynamical systems (produces fixed points, limit cycles, chaos). chapter, solve numerically R two classic games visualize resulting dynamics.

heading: 20.2.1 The replicator equation
text:
Consider large population individuals play one \(n\) strategies. Let \(x_i\) fraction population playing strategy \(\), \(\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)\) lies simplex \(\Delta^{n-1} = \{ \mathbf{x} \geq 0 : \sum_i x_i = 1 \}\).fitness strategy \(\) current population :
\[\begin{equation}
f_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_j a_{ij} x_j = (\mathbf{x})_i
$$
$= (a_{ij})$ payoff matrix. **average fitness** :
$$
\bar{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_i x_i f_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{x}
$$

**replicator equation** :
$$
\dot{x}_i = x_i \bigl( f_i(\mathbf{x}) - \bar{f}(\mathbf{x}) \bigr), \quad = 1, \ldots, n
\tag{17.3}
\end{equation}\]interpretation elegant: strategy grows fitness exceeds population average, shrinks falls . rate change proportional current frequency \(x_i\) (rare strategies change slowly absolute terms) fitness advantage \(f_i - \bar{f}\).

heading: 20.2.2 Key properties
text: Simplex invariance: \(\mathbf{x}(0) \\Delta^{n-1}\), \(\mathbf{x}(t) \\Delta^{n-1}\) \(t \geq 0\). Frequencies always sum one.Rest points: Every vertex simplex (pure-strategy population) rest point. Interior rest points correspond mixed Nash equilibria underlying game.Folk theorem: two-strategy games, every ESS asymptotically stable rest point replicator dynamics, every stable rest point Nash equilibrium.

heading: 20.2.3 Two example games
20.3.1 Solving with run_replicator()
text: helper function run_replicator() (defined R/replicator.R) wraps deSolve::ode() integrate replicator equation. takes payoff matrix, initial frequencies, time grid, returning data frame strategy frequencies time.
code:
A_rps <- matrix(c( 0, -1,  1,
                    1,  0, -1,
                   -1,  1,  0), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)

A_hd <- matrix(c(-1, 2,
                   0, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)

heading: 20.3.2 Simplex coordinate transform
text: plot three-strategy dynamics two-dimensional ternary diagram, project simplex Cartesian coordinates using standard transform:

heading: 20.3.3 Running RPS dynamics from multiple initial conditions
code:
set.seed(42)
n_traj <- 6
initial_conditions <- map(seq_len(n_traj), function(i) {
  raw <- runif(3)
  raw / sum(raw)
})

rps_trajectories <- map_dfr(seq_along(initial_conditions), function(k) {
  x0 <- initial_conditions[[k]]
  out <- run_replicator(A_rps, x0, times = seq(0, 40, by = 0.05))
  coords <- simplex_to_cartesian(out[, 2], out[, 3], out[, 4])
  coords$trajectory <- factor(k)
  coords$time <- out$time
  coords
})

heading: 20.3.4 Running Hawk-Dove dynamics
code:
hd_initials <- c(0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9)

hd_trajectories <- map_dfr(hd_initials, function(p) {
  x0 <- c(p, 1 - p)
  out <- run_replicator(A_hd, x0, times = seq(0, 20, by = 0.05))
  df <- as_tibble(out)
  names(df) <- c("time", "Hawk", "Dove")
  df$initial <- paste0("x0 = ", p)
  df
})

heading: 20.4 Worked example
text: trace replicator dynamics specific initial conditions games.

heading: 20.4.1 Rock-Paper-Scissors
text:
Starting \(\mathbf{x}_0 = (0.6, 0.3, 0.1)\) — population dominated Rock:frequencies cycle endlessly around interior equilibrium \((1/3, 1/3, 1/3)\). Rock starts high, attracting Paper players can exploit ; Paper rises, attracting Scissors; Scissors rises, attracting Rock — cycle repeats. orbit never converges interior equilibrium center (neutrally stable), attractor.
Figure 20.1: Replicator dynamics Rock-Paper-Scissors two-dimensional simplex. Six trajectories random initial conditions cycle around interior equilibrium (1/3, 1/3, 1/3). orbits closed curves — system never converges, reflecting non-transitive dominance structure game.
code:
x0_rps <- c(0.6, 0.3, 0.1)
out_rps <- run_replicator(A_rps, x0_rps, times = seq(0, 40, by = 0.1))
names(out_rps) <- c("time", "Rock", "Paper", "Scissors")

cat("RPS dynamics from x0 = (0.6, 0.3, 0.1):\n\n")#> RPS dynamics from x0 = (0.6, 0.3, 0.1):
cat(sprintf("  t = %2.0f:  Rock = %.3f  Paper = %.3f  Scissors = %.3f\n",
            0, x0_rps[1], x0_rps[2], x0_rps[3]))#>   t =  0:  Rock = 0.600  Paper = 0.300  Scissors = 0.100

Hawk-Dove dynamics illustrate core result evolutionary game theory: ESS global attractor replicator dynamics. matter population starts (long strategies present), converges mixed equilibrium Hawks Doves coexist. stark contrast RPS, dynamics cycle forever.

heading: 20.4.3 Comparing the two games
text: difference two games reflects deep structural distinction:Hawk-Dove symmetric \(2 \times 2\) payoff matrix interior ESS. replicator dynamics Lyapunov function (quantity strictly decreases along trajectories), guaranteeing convergence.Rock-Paper-Scissors skew-symmetric payoff matrix. quantity \(H(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 x_2 x_3\) conserved along trajectories (constant motion), producing closed orbits rather convergence.shows replicator equation’s behavior depends qualitatively game’s payoff structure — theme revisit studying evolutionarily stable strategies 21.

heading: 20.5 Extensions
text: Asymmetric replicator dynamics: players occupy different roles (e.g., owner vs. intruder), dynamics involve two coupled replicator equations, one population. leads higher-dimensional phase spaces.Mutation selection: Adding small mutation rate \(\mu\) transforms replicator equation replicator-mutator equation: \(\dot{x}_i = \sum_j x_j f_j Q_{ji} - x_i \bar{f}\), \(Q\) mutation matrix. can turn centers spirals.Discrete-time replicator: discrete-time version \(x_i' = x_i f_i / \bar{f}\) different stability properties can exhibit chaos even low dimensions.Connection spatial models: well-mixed replicator dynamics serve baseline spatial models 19. spatial structure matters, local frequency-dependent selection can sustain strategies replicator equation predicts go extinct.comprehensive treatment, see Hofbauer & Sigmund (1998). connection replicator dynamics learning, see Fudenberg & Levine (1998).
