heading: D Exercise Solutions
text: Solutions end--chapter exercises.appendix provides brief solution sketches selected exercises Part (Chapters 1–8). goal offer enough guidance check reasoning without removing learning opportunity. Full, runnable solutions available book’s online repository.

heading: Chapter 1: What is a Game?
text:
Exercise 1 — Identify game. Choose strategic interaction everyday life identify four building blocks.Many valid answers exist. One example: two roommates deciding whether clean shared kitchen.Players: Roommate Roommate B.Actions: Clean Don’t Clean (player).Payoffs: clean kitchen benefits , cleaning costly. one cleans, person bears full cost enjoy result — structurally Prisoner’s Dilemma.Information: Simultaneous — neither observes ’s choice deciding.key criterion mutual dependence: player’s best action depends .Exercise 2 — Payoff heatmap. Create payoff matrix Battle Sexes.payoff matrices :\[
= \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}
\]Use make_payoff_df() strategies c("Opera", "Football") players. heatmap show high payoffs diagonal (coordination) zeros diagonal (miscoordination). Unlike Prisoner’s Dilemma, dominant strategy — diagonal cells Nash equilibria.

heading: Chapter 2: Normal-Form Games
text: Exercise 1 — Identifying dominance. Find strictly weakly dominated strategies 3x3 game.Player 1, compare row every row across columns:Row 2 yields payoffs \((3, 2, 5)\). Row 1 yields \((2, 4, 1)\). Neither strictly dominates (Row 1 better column 2, Row 2 columns 1 3).Row 3 yields \((1, 3, 2)\). Row 2 gives \(3 > 1\), \(2 < 3\), \(5 > 2\) — , strict dominance.Check pairwise comparisons systematically. weak dominance, look rows one least good every column strictly better least one. Apply logic Player 2’s columns using matrix \(B\).Exercise 2 — IESDS practice. Apply IESDS 3x3 game Exercise 1.Apply IESDS step step:Check strictly dominated strategies original game eliminate .reduced game, check newly dominated strategies.Repeat elimination possible.order elimination matter strict dominance (order independence). Document step showing reduced matrix. game reduces single cell, dominance solvable.

heading: Chapter 3: Nash Equilibrium
text:
Exercise 1 — Stag Hunt equilibria. Find Nash equilibria Stag Hunt.two pure-strategy NE (Stag, Stag) payoffs \((4, 4)\) (Hare, Hare) payoffs \((3, 3)\). Verify checking player can profitably deviate.mixed NE, use indifference principle. Player 2 plays Stag probability \(q\):\[
4q + 0(1-q) = 3q + 3(1-q) \implies 4q = 3 \implies q = 3/4
\]symmetry, \(p = 3/4\) well. mixed NE \((3/4, 3/4)\) expected payoff \(3\) player.Payoff-dominant: (Stag, Stag) yields \(4 > 3\).Risk-dominant: (Hare, Hare), Hare best response mixed NE probability safer uncertainty opponent.Exercise 3 — Best-response plot Chicken. Plot best-response correspondences.Set indifference conditions player using Chicken payoffs. Player 1’s expected payoff Swerve equals payoff Straight indifference point. Solve critical mixing probability plot best-response functions unit square \([0, 1]^2\). plot reveal three intersection points: two corners (pure NE) one interior (mixed NE).

heading: Chapter 4: Mixed Strategies
text:
Exercise 1 — Asymmetric Matching Pennies. Compute mixed NE Player 1 receives 3 (Heads, Heads).Payoff matrices:\[
= \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}
\]Player 2’s indifference (making Player 1 indifferent governed \(B\)):\[
-q + (1-q) = q - (1-q) \implies q^* = 1/2
\]Player 1’s indifference (making Player 2 indifferent governed \(\)):\[
3p - (1-p) = -p + (1-p) \implies 4p - 1 = -2p + 1 \implies p^* = 1/3
\]asymmetry Player 1’s payoff changes Player 1’s mixing probability (Player 2’s). “-payoff effect” — player’s equilibrium mixing probability determined opponent’s payoffs, .Exercise 4 — Rock-Paper-Scissors. Argue unique mixed NE assigns 1/3 action.symmetry game, NE assigns different probabilities three actions, permuting labels yield another NE different probabilities identical structure — contradicting uniqueness. Therefore unique symmetric NE must assign equal probability \(1/3\) action.formally: indifference conditions pair actions identical cyclic symmetry payoff matrix, mixing probabilities must equal. Since sum 1, \(1/3\).

heading: Chapter 5: Extensive-Form Games
text: Exercise 1 — Ultimatum game. Find NE SPE simplified ultimatum game.game tree Player 1 choosing Fair Greedy, Player 2 choosing Accept Reject.Backward induction: Player 2’s subgames, accepting strictly better rejecting (5 > 0 2 > 0). Player 2 accepts subgames. Anticipating , Player 1 chooses Greedy (payoff 8 > 5). SPE (Greedy; Accept, Accept).Nash equilibria: additional NE Player 2 threatens reject Greedy offer. example, (Fair; Accept, Reject) NE Player 1’s deviation Greedy yields 0 given Player 2’s strategy. However, involves non-credible threat — Player 2 actually reject payoff 2 — backward induction eliminates .Exercise 3 — Non-credible threats. incumbent’s Fight payoff 2?Fight payoff 2, incumbent now weakly prefers fighting accommodating (strictly prefers Accommodate yields less 2). subgame entry, Fight least good Accommodate, threat fight credible.SPE changes: entrant, anticipating fight, may prefer stay . illustrates key insight — whether threat credible depends payoffs subgame, threat .

heading: Chapter 6: Bayesian Games
text:
Exercise 1 — Modified signaling costs. separating equilibrium survive \(c_L = 3\)?separating equilibrium, High type chooses Education Low type . Low type must want deviate Education. incentive compatibility condition Low type :\[
\text{Payoff(Education)} \geq \text{Payoff(Education)} - c_L
\]\(c_L = 3\), check whether Low type’s gain perceived High (receiving High-type wage) exceeds education cost. wage differential , say, \(8 - 2 = 6\) \(c_L = 3 < 6\), Low type profitably deviate Education, breaking separation.minimum \(c_L\) sustains separation wage differential: Low type must find education costly mimic High type.Exercise 2 — Pooling equilibrium. Can types pool Education?pooling equilibrium Education, employer observes signal pays expected productivity: \(E[\theta] = P(H) \cdot \theta_H + P(L) \cdot \theta_L\). Neither type deviates -path belief worker chooses Education sufficiently pessimistic (e.g., employer believes educating worker Low type probability 1). beliefs, education yields Low-type wage minus education cost, worse pooling wage types. pessimistic -path beliefs permitted Bayes’ rule (imposes constraint equilibrium path).

heading: Chapter 7: Repeated Games
text:
Exercise 1 — Critical discount factor. Compute \(\delta^*\) Grim Trigger \(T = 8, R = 5, P = 2, S = 0\).Grim Trigger, cooperation yields \(V_C = R / (1 - \delta) = 5 / (1 - \delta)\).one-shot deviation yields \(T\) today \(P\) forever: \(V_D = T + \delta P / (1 - \delta) = 8 + 2\delta / (1 - \delta)\).Cooperation sustained \(V_C \geq V_D\):\[
\frac{5}{1 - \delta} \geq 8 + \frac{2\delta}{1 - \delta}
\]\[
5 \geq 8(1 - \delta) + 2\delta = 8 - 6\delta
\]\[
6\delta \geq 3 \implies \delta^* = 1/2
\]Verify R computing \(V_C\) \(V_D\) \(\delta = 0.5\) confirming equal.Exercise 3 — Folk theorem region. Plot feasible individually rational region.feasible payoff set convex hull four payoff profiles: \((4, 4)\), \((0, 6)\), \((6, 0)\), \((1, 1)\). individually rational region requires player’s payoff least minmax value. 2x2 game, compute player’s minmax payoff finding worst opponent can guarantee.Folk theorem region intersection: feasible payoffs dominate players’ minmax values. Plot convex hull polygon shade region minmax thresholds.

heading: Chapter 8: Cooperative Game Theory
text:
Exercise 1 — Asymmetric savings. Compute Shapley value modified water treatment game.\(v(\{\}) = v(\{B\}) = v(\{C\}) = 0\), \(v(\{,B\}) = 50\), \(v(\{,C\}) = 30\), \(v(\{B,C\}) = 40\), \(v(\{,B,C\}) = 80\).Shapley value player \(\) :\[
\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq N \setminus \{\}} \frac{|S|! \, (n - |S| - 1)!}{n!} \left[ v(S \cup \{\}) - v(S) \right]
\]3-player game, enumerate \(3! = 6\) orderings compute player’s marginal contribution ordering. Average across orderings.example, ordering \((, B, C)\): \(\) contributes \(v(\{\}) - v(\emptyset) = 0\), \(B\) contributes \(v(\{,B\}) - v(\{\}) = 50\), \(C\) contributes \(v(\{,B,C\}) - v(\{,B\}) = 30\).Repeat six orderings average. Municipality benefits large coalitions B; Municipality B valuable partner overall.Exercise 4 — Glove game. Write characteristic function, compute Shapley value, find core.Characteristic function: \(v(S) = \min(\text{left gloves } S, \text{right gloves } S)\). \(v(\{1\}) = v(\{2\}) = v(\{3\}) = 0\), \(v(\{1,2\}) = 0\) (two left, right), \(v(\{1,3\}) = v(\{2,3\}) = 1\), \(v(\{1,2,3\}) = 1\).Shapley value: enumerate \(3! = 6\) orderings. Player 3 (sole right-glove holder) pivotal 4 6 orderings, yielding \(\phi_3 = 4/6 = 2/3\). symmetry, \(\phi_1 = \phi_2 = 1/6\).Core: core requires \(x_1 + x_3 \geq 1\), \(x_2 + x_3 \geq 1\), \(x_1 + x_2 \geq 0\), \(x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 1\), \(x_i \geq 0\). first two constraints efficiency: \(x_3 \geq 1 - x_1\) \(x_3 \geq 1 - x_2\). Since \(x_1 + x_2 = 1 - x_3\), constraints give \(x_3 = 1\) \(x_1 = x_2 = 0\). core single point \((0, 0, 1)\). Shapley value \((1/6, 1/6, 2/3)\) lie core.

heading: Additional selected solutions
text:
brief hints exercises students frequently ask .Chapter 2, Exercise 4 — story matrix. Two firms choose Invest Stay.payoff matrix :\[
= B = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{pmatrix}
\]rows/columns (Invest, Stay). Check dominance: Invest, Stay yields 4 > 1 (role firm); Stay, Stay yields 3 > 1. Stay strictly dominates Invest players, (Stay, Stay) payoffs \((3, 3)\) unique NE. Prisoner’s Dilemma: mutual investment yield \((5, 5)\) dominant strategy leads Pareto-inferior outcome.Chapter 4, Exercise 2 — Inspection game. Find mixed NE Worker-Employer game.Payoff matrices (Worker row player, Employer column player):\[
= \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\]rows (Work, Shirk) columns (Trust, Monitor).pure-strategy NE exists (verify checking best responses). mixed NE, let Worker play Work probability \(p\) Employer play Trust probability \(q\).Employer’s indifference (using \(\)): \(2q + 1(1-q) = 3q + 0(1-q)\), \(q + 1 = 3q\), giving \(q^* = 1/2\).Worker’s indifference (using \(B\)): \(3p + 0(1-p) = 2p + 1(1-p)\), \(3p = p + 1\), giving \(p^* = 1/2\).Increasing penalty caught shirking (lowering \(A_{2,2}\) 0) change \(p^*\) equilibrium — changes employer’s mixing probability \(q^*\) instead. another instance “-payoff” paradox indifference principle.Chapter 5, Exercise 4 — SPE vs. NE counting. Count equilibria ultimatum game.normal form 2 strategies Player 1 (Fair, Greedy) 4 strategies Player 2 (Accept/Accept, Accept/Reject, Reject/Accept, Reject/Reject — one decision Player 1’s possible offers).Pure-strategy NE: find cells players play best response. three NE:(Greedy; Accept, Accept) — payoffs (8, 2).(Fair; Accept, Reject) — payoffs (5, 5).(Fair; Reject, Reject) — payoffs (5, 5).NE 1 subgame perfect, NE 2 3 Player 2’s plan reject Greedy offer sequentially rational (2 > 0). Thus SPE \(\subset\) NE strictly.Chapter 7, Exercise 2 — Tit--Tat critical discount factor. Derive \(\delta^*\) TFT.TFT \(T = 5, R = 3, P = 1, S = 0\): cooperation yields \(V_C = 3 / (1 - \delta)\).deviation yields \(T = 5\) today. TFT retaliates next period (defects ), deviator gets \(S = 0\) period 2. TFT returns cooperation (copies opponent’s last action), period 3 onward deviator gets \(R = 3\) — deviator also returns cooperation. one-shot deviation payoff stream :\[
V_D = 5 + 0 \cdot \delta + 3 \cdot \frac{\delta^2}{1 - \delta}
\]Setting \(V_C \geq V_D\):\[
\frac{3}{1 - \delta} \geq 5 + \frac{3\delta^2}{1 - \delta}
\]\[
3 \geq 5(1 - \delta) + 3\delta^2 = 5 - 5\delta + 3\delta^2
\]\[
3\delta^2 - 5\delta + 2 \leq 0
\]Solving: \(\delta = \frac{5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 24}}{6} = \frac{5 \pm 1}{6}\), \(\delta \[2/3, 1]\). critical discount factor \(\delta^* = 2/3\), higher Grim Trigger threshold (\(1/2\) payoffs), reflecting TFT’s milder punishment.

heading: E Glossary
text:
Key terms definitions used throughout book.
Action
: choice available player decision point. simultaneous game, terms “action” “strategy” often used interchangeably.Agent-based model
computational model autonomous agents interact according specified rules, producing emergent macro-level behaviour. See 17.
Backward induction
solution method extensive-form games perfect information. Starting terminal nodes working backwards, player’s optimal action determined every decision node. resulting strategy profile subgame perfect equilibrium. See 6.
Battle Sexes
2x2 coordination game two pure-strategy Nash equilibria one mixed equilibrium. players prefer coordinate disagree outcome better.
Bayesian game
game players private information (types) drawn known prior distribution. Players maximise expected payoffs given beliefs opponents’ types. See 7.
Bayesian Nash equilibrium (BNE)
strategy profile Bayesian game type player maximises expected payoff given prior distribution opponents’ types strategies.
Best response
strategy \(s_i^*\) maximises player \(\)’s payoff given strategies players. Nash equilibrium profile every player’s strategy best response.
Characteristic function
cooperative game theory, function \(v: 2^N \\mathbb{R}\) assigns worth every coalition \(S \subseteq N\). See 9.
Chicken (Hawk-Dove)
2x2 anti-coordination game modelling brinkmanship. player prefers yield. two asymmetric pure-strategy NE one mixed NE.
Common knowledge
fact common knowledge players know , players know players know , ad infinitum. structure game typically assumed common knowledge.
Cooperative game theory
branch game theory studies coalitions players can achieve joint gains allocated. Focuses outcomes rather strategies. See 9.
Core
set feasible payoff allocations cooperative game coalition can improve upon. allocation core subset players can better breaking away.
Discount factor
parameter \(\delta \[0, 1)\) representing weight player places future payoffs relative current payoffs. Central repeated game analysis. See 8.
Dominant strategy
strategy yields payoff least high alternative regardless opponents’ actions (weak dominance) strictly higher (strict dominance).
Evolutionary stable strategy (ESS)
strategy , prevalent population, invaded rare mutant strategy. Formally, strategy \(s^*\) ESS best response , alternative best response, strictly better mutant mutant . See ??.
Extensive form
representation game tree, specifying order moves, information available decision point, payoffs terminal nodes. See 6.
Feasible payoff set
convex hull achievable payoff vectors game. repeated games, folk theorem concerns subset feasible payoffs individually rational.
Folk theorem
family results stating infinitely repeated games sufficiently patient players (\(\delta\) close 1), feasible individually rational payoff vector can sustained Nash equilibrium outcome. See 8.
Game tree
tree representation extensive-form game, nodes representing decision points edges representing actions.
Grim Trigger
strategy repeated games cooperates opponent defects, defects forever. simplest strategy can sustain cooperation via threat permanent punishment.
Imperfect information
game imperfect information least one player observe previous moves making decision. Represented information sets game tree.
Incomplete information
game incomplete information players uncertain aspect game, players’ payoffs types. Modelled Bayesian game.
Individually rational payoff
minimum payoff player can guarantee regardless opponents’ strategies (minmax value). repeated games, folk theorem applies payoffs threshold.
Information set
extensive-form game, collection decision nodes among player distinguish. player must choose action nodes information set.
Matching Pennies
2x2 zero-sum game one player wins outcomes match wins differ. unique Nash equilibrium mixed strategies.
Mechanism design
“inverse” game theory: designing rules game (mechanism) self-interested players produce desired outcome equilibrium. See 33.
Minmax value
lowest payoff opponents can force player, equivalently, highest payoff player can guarantee worst-case opponent behaviour.
Mixed strategy
probability distribution player’s pure strategies. player randomises among actions according specified probabilities. See 5.
Monte Carlo method
computational technique uses random sampling estimate quantities simulate systems. Used throughout Part III game simulations. See 16.
Nash equilibrium (NE)
strategy profile player can increase payoff unilaterally changing strategy. Every finite game least one Nash equilibrium (possibly mixed strategies). See 4.
Normal form (strategic form)
representation simultaneous game matrix payoffs. row corresponds strategy one player, column strategy . See 3.
Pareto optimal (Pareto efficient)
outcome Pareto optimal alternative outcome makes least one player strictly better without making player worse . Prisoner’s Dilemma equilibrium famously Pareto inefficient.
Payoff matrix
matrix encoding players’ payoffs every combination strategies normal-form game.
Perfect information
game perfect information every player, making decision, observes previously taken actions. Chess canonical example.
Prisoner’s Dilemma
2x2 game player dominant strategy defect, yet mutual cooperation yields higher payoff . unique NE Pareto inefficient.
Pure strategy
deterministic plan action specifies exactly action player take every decision point.
Replicator dynamics
system differential equations describing proportions strategies population change time based relative fitness. See 20.
Shapley value
unique allocation cooperative game theory distributes total value among players according average marginal contribution across possible orderings. See 9.
Signaling game
dynamic Bayesian game informed player (sender) takes observable action convey information uninformed player (receiver). See 7.
Stag Hunt
2x2 coordination game two pure-strategy NE: one payoff-dominant (hunt Stag) one risk-dominant (hunt Hare). models tension cooperation safety.
Strategy profile
specification one strategy every player game. \(n\)-player game strategy profile \((s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n)\).
Subgame
portion extensive-form game starts singleton information set, contains successors, break information set.
Subgame perfect equilibrium (SPE)
strategy profile constitutes Nash equilibrium every subgame original game. Found backward induction games perfect information. See 6.
Support (mixed strategy)
set pure strategies assigned positive probability mixed strategy. Nash equilibrium, every strategy support must yield expected payoff.
Tit--Tat (TFT)
strategy repeated games cooperates first round, copies opponent’s previous action. Famously successful Axelrod’s tournaments. See 18.
Zero-sum game
game sum players’ payoffs zero (constant) every outcome. One player’s gain exactly another’s loss. Matching Pennies canonical example.
